description: Compact index for AI queries. For full details, load {path}/summary.md or {path}/metadata.json

items.t: RIP Robert Murray-Smith (1963 - 2025)
topics: Announcement of Robert Murray-Smith's death by suicide
Robert's grief over the loss of his wife Patty
Robert's declining physical health and its contribution to his depression
Robert's 11-year relationship with his YouTube community
Memorial donation drive for CALM (Campaign Against Living Miserably), a men's mental health charity
Planned future "best of" compilation video

items.t: 2440 My Last Video
topics: Announcement of the channel's end after 10–11 years
Gratitude to subscribers, members, and supporters

Confirmation that all past videos will remain publicly available
A personal farewell and fond goodbye

items.t: 2439 Archimedean Spiral Gears - A Freecad Tutorial

items.t: 2432 Hemi Spherical Gears

items.t: 2438 The Hypocycloid Clamp - Compass or Robot Hand?

items.t: 2437 The Hypocycloid Hyperdrive - 1500 to 1 With Only 3 Gears
topics: What a cycloid (hypocycloid) drive is and how its gear profile differs from standard involute gears

Axle (20 mm diameter)
Clips (used to retain the axle assembly)

items.t: 2435 How I Do Projects
topics: Sources of project ideas (viewer questions, museum visits, desire to learn a new skill)

Workflow of combining parts from multiple CAD programs into one final model

Iterative prototyping philosophy: print, find mistakes, correct, reprint
Anti-procrastination philosophy: jump in, accept failure as part of learning, stay job-focused
materials: **PLA** — primary filament used for all prototyping due to low cost and ease of printing
**PETG** — used occasionally for more demanding prints
**Carbon fiber-filled filament** — used for final versions of proven designs (~£30–50/roll)
**Wood-filled and metal-filled filament** — mentioned as exotic options tried
**Transparent (clear) filament** — mentioned among materials used
**TPU** — flexible filament, mentioned as part of his wider experience
**ABS** — mentioned but rarely used

items.t: Impossible Gears
topics: Gear ratio fundamentals: pitch circle, tooth count as shorthand, drive vs. driven relationships
The "impossible" premise: why same-sized gears were assumed to always yield a 1:1 ratio
Mechanism 1: Spur gears with a lever/bar arm producing a 2:1 ratio and simultaneous power split across two axles
Mechanism 2: Three identical bevel gears in a fixed/rotating arrangement producing a 2:1 ratio
Mechanism 3: Three bevel gears with an additional modular stage producing a 3:1 ratio (and back-drivability)
Mechanism 4: Same-sized spur gears in a rotating cage with a fixed gear producing a 2:1 ratio
Mechanism 5: Trammel of Archimedes (ellipsograph / "do nothing machine") incorporated into a gear mechanism for a 2:1 ratio
Mathematical explanation: epicycloids, rolling circles, and why the path length constraint is satisfied
Connection to planetary gear systems and the general formula for epicycloid lobes: **n + 1** (where n = ratio of fixed to rolling circle radii)
Leibniz's philosophical concept of the monad as an aside on infinitesimal points
materials: 3D-printed PLA parts (designed in Tinkercad, files released on Thingiverse)
Small clips and pegs (press-fit fasteners for assembly)
Adhesive/glue (used to fix certain gears rigidly to their axles)
Silicone oil (applied to the Trammel of Archimedes mechanism to reduce friction and allow smooth operation)

items.t: 2436 A Variable Stroke Piston

How eccentricity determines stroke length (zero at center, maximum at full offset)
materials: PLA (polylactic acid) filament — used for all 3D-printed parts
Cyanoacrylate (crazy glue/super glue) — used to bond PLA parts; noted as bonding stronger than the PLA itself
Two-part accelerator spray — used with the cyanoacrylate for immediate setting

Glue (used to fix P-shaped housings and caps)

items.t: Putting In A Simple Solar System - Omnibus

items.t: 2409 Friction Drives

**3 mm basswood** — also cut on the laser cutter as an alternative material test
**PLA / 3D print filament** (implied) — used for sun, moon, earth bodies, feet, and pillars
**Superglue** — used throughout assembly to fix gears, feet, and planetary bodies
**Battery box** — under £1, sourced from Amazon; powers the motor
**Small plastic gearbox motor** — ~£2, sourced from Amazon
**2 mm diameter × 40 mm steel bar** — used as the motor axle

items.t: 2284 The XTool P2 CO2 Laser Cutter - A New Way Of Working #xtool #p2

XTool's community and support ecosystem (compared to Arduino's model)

**Wood** — cited as cuttable up to 18 mm in a single pass
**Deionized water** — used as the primary laser tube coolant
**Antifreeze** — mixed with deionized water according to a temperature table to prevent freezing
**CO2 (fire extinguisher)** — used in the optional integrated fire safety kit

items.t: 2283 A Hypocycloid Differential
topics: The standard automotive differential (ring and pinion type) as context
Other differential types: epicyclic (sun and planet), spheria, and hypocycloid
Historical use of differentials — traced back to the Antikythera mechanism (~80 BC)
Gear tooth profiles: involute (most common) vs. cycloid
How cycloid gears work and their niche uses (e.g., watches)
The hypocycloid gear mechanism sourced from Otvinta
Modification of a hypocycloid gear into a differential (adding cogs to ring and output gears, mounting in a cradle)
Demonstration of differential behaviour: same-direction input = fast output; opposing-direction input = slow output
Applications of hypocycloid gears in robotics (thin profile, high gear ratio, high torque, low friction)
General definition of a differential: any gear arrangement that splits or combines one shaft's power into two (or vice versa) with independent, variable ratios
materials: 3D-printed plastic gears (implied by the fabrication context and Otvinta design source)

items.t: 2282 A Different Kind Of Differential For Wind Turbines
topics: Savonius turbine design and its inherent efficiency limitations
The edge-on blade problem and why spiral stacking is a common workaround
Research finding that three-bladed Savonius is the practical maximum for efficiency gain via extra blades
Side-by-side rotor arrangement as an alternative to spiral stacking
The slip problem with standard open car differentials
Planetary gear systems (sun gear, ring gear, planet gears, gear carrier) and how they work in drills
Configuring a planetary gear set as an epicyclic differential by allowing all three elements to rotate
Demonstration that same-direction inputs cancel output; opposite-direction inputs sum to produce greater output
Cycloid drive as an alternative mechanism for creating a differential
Reference to earlier video 2197 where the differential was first built
materials: None mentioned.

items.t: 2281 Making Stronger Wind Turbines
topics: Weakness of FDM 3D-printed parts due to layer grain orientation in turbine blades
Superglue coating as a temporary fix for blade brittleness
Introduction of the gyroid lattice (gyroid infill) and its isotropic strength properties
Comparison test: grid infill vs. gyroid infill at 10% infill density, single-layer shell
Weight difference between the two blade types (~14–14.5 g gyroid vs. ~16 g grid)
Break-force results: 698 g (gyroid) vs. 639 g (grid) — ~9% strength increase
References to external testing by CNC Kitchen and 3D Printer Academy
Scientific research citing up to 40% strength increase with gyroid infills
materials: **Superglue (cyanoacrylate)** — applied as a coating to strengthen FDM-printed blades
**PLA or similar FDM filament** (implied) — used to print the turbine blades

items.t: 2280 Build A Pulse Motor - A One Day Project For 3D Printing

**Smaller neodymium magnets (1 cm × 0.5 cm × 1 mm)** — folded in pairs to create north/south orientation; stacked two layers deep in the flywheel
**Superglue** — used throughout for bonding 3D-printed parts

**Household sewing needle** — forms the low-friction pivot point for the axle tip
**Trimming knife blade (steel strip)** — provides the bearing surface the needle tip rests on
**Battery (type unspecified)** — primary power source for the pendulum drive unit
**Supercapacitor** — mentioned as an alternative power source for longer run times and more impressive performance

**3D printer filament** — used to fabricate the demonstration rings and printed gyroid models

items.t: 2275 The Pelton Wind Turbine

items.t: A Sincere Thank You

Confirming he will continue making YouTube videos

items.t: In Memorium Of  Patti As She Teaches A Simple Science Rocket Experiment
topics: Memorial tribute to Patti Murray-Smith, Robert's wife and a teacher
Simple home science experiment: acid-base bottle rocket
Chemistry of the vinegar-bicarbonate of soda reaction (producing CO₂ gas)
Building a stable tripod launch stand from everyday materials
Safety practices when handling chemicals at home
Encouraging experimentation and hands-on science for young people
materials: **Empty plastic water/drinks bottle** — serves as the rocket body; recycling encouraged
**Vinegar (acetic acid)** — weak acid; bottle filled halfway; noted that brown vinegar makes more mess and premium vinegars are unnecessary
**Bicarbonate of soda (baking soda / sodium bicarbonate)** — alkaline reactant; approximately one tablespoon used; explicitly distinguished from baking powder
**Tissue paper / tracing paper** — used to wrap the bicarbonate of soda into a packet; tracing paper found to work best through experimentation
**Cork or rubber bung** — seals the bottle; a chemistry flask bung used as substitute for a wine cork
**Masking tape** — preferred over cellophane tape for ease of handling; used to secure tripod legs
**Three pens, pencils, or sticks** — form the tripod launch stand
**Small funnel** — from a basic home lab kit; used to pour vinegar
**Safety glasses** — recommended whenever working with any chemicals
**Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — the gas produced by the reaction, which propels the rocket
**Water and salt** — also noted as byproducts of the acid-base reaction

items.t: Man In A Shed - The Car Special  - Episode 9
topics: History of electric vehicles from 1828 (Robert Anderson) through the Edison-Ford project to the modern EV revival
Why EVs died (battery limitations, cheap oil, cheaper petrol cars) and why they returned (consumer demand, government policy)
Current EV shortcomings: cost, charging infrastructure, insurance, energy source still largely fossil fuels
Hydrogen generation via aluminium + potassium hydroxide (the Yeno method), including live demonstration
The Francois Cornish patent (US 4,702,894) for an aluminium-wire/water hydrogen generator, and BMW's trials
Ammonia as a combustion fuel: history (X-15 rocket plane, Belgian WWII buses), GAC/Toyota production ammonia car, infrastructure advantages
Zinc-chlorine flow batteries: Gulf Western's 1970s system, Stanford's 2021 microporous carbon advance, 2023 manganese dioxide cathode paper, and the NanoFlowcell production car
Wind-powered vehicles: Greenbird, Blackbird, Savonius rotor model build, Dutch inventor Oscar DEA's turbine-charged EV
The "water car" — clarifying what it is and is not, history of fraud cases, and Graneau's cavitation-based approach as the only legitimate candidate
materials: **Potassium hydroxide (KOH), 5 M concentrated solution** — electrolyte/reactant in the Yeno method hydrogen generation demo
**Aluminium (foil and wire)** — reacts with KOH to produce hydrogen; also used as the metal in Cornish's patented system
**Potassium aluminate** — reaction product of aluminium + KOH
**Hydrogen (H₂)** — gas produced by Yeno method; fuel for hydrogen cars and the Cornish BMW trial
**Nickel-iron battery (Edison's "nickeline" battery)** — intended for Ford-Edison EV project; had high internal resistance issues
**Lead-acid battery** — the limiting battery technology of early EVs
**Ammonia (NH₃)** — zero-CO₂ combustion fuel (burns to N₂ + H₂O); used in X-15 aircraft, Belgian WWII buses, University of Michigan cross-country drive, and GAC/Toyota 2 L saloon car
**Liquid oxygen / hydrogen (promoters)** — added at ~3% to make ammonia burn as fast as conventional fuel
**Zinc chloride (ZnCl₂)** — electrolyte in zinc-chlorine flow battery
**Chlorine (Cl₂)** — cathode reactant in zinc-chlorine battery; stored as chlorine hydrate
**Chlorine hydrate (Cl₂·8H₂O)** — ice-like frozen storage form of chlorine in Gulf Western battery
**Microporous / activated carbon** — used by Stanford (2021) to trap chlorine (and other halogens) in battery cathode
**Manganese dioxide (MnO₂)** — cathode coating in 2023 paper that improved zinc-chlorine battery reaction kinetics
**Sodium / lithium** — metals substituted for zinc in Stanford's updated chlorine battery work
**Sodium chloride (saltwater)** — claimed working fluid of NanoFlowcell's "Quantino" flow-battery car
**HHO / Brown's gas** — partial electrolysis product, clarified as *not* the basis of a true "water car"
**Metal hydride** — mentioned in context of the 2008 Japanese Genipax claim

items.t: 2224 Energy Storage Game Changer - The Eindhoven Heat Battery
topics: Historical context: the 1956 World Symposium on Practical Use of Solar Energy (Phoenix, Arizona) and the *Popular Mechanics* report
Maria Telkes and her pioneering solar thermal house using Glauber's salt as a phase-change material

Modular cell design, 70 kWh units, and a 200 kWh stack capacity

Real-world trials: four homes (two in Netherlands, one in Poland, one in France)
European development funding and the goal of transitioning 3.5 million homes off gas

**Urea** — principal component of urine; endothermic dissolution in water, used to create the cold side of a Peltier device
**Potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)** — selected by the Eindhoven team after testing hundreds of materials; withstands repeated wet/dry cycling
**Graphite / carbon** — mixed with potassium carbonate in the Eindhoven battery to improve heat transfer
**Graphite foil** — used by Robert as a thermal contact pad between components in his bench demonstrations

History of water wheel improvement: Jean-Charles de Borda and Jean-Victor Poncelet in late 18th/early 19th century France
How curved blades eliminate bounce-back and improve efficiency
Comparison of wheel types: undershot, overshot, Poncelet, Pelton, Kaplan turbine, Sagebien, and Zuppinger wheels
Practical manufacturability and cost advantages of the Poncelet wheel over Pelton/Kaplan designs
Modern revival efforts, including a boom-arm mounted wheel by Naring Industries
The Poncelet wheel at Groudle Glen on the Isle of Man as a surviving example

items.t: 2210 The Liam F1 - Spirals And Wind Turbines
topics: The four main types of spirals found in nature (Fibonacci, logarithmic, golden ratio, Archimedes) and their practical equivalences from an engineering perspective
Savonius wind turbines as the simplest baseline turbine design
A research paper applying Fibonacci spiral geometry to Savonius-type blades, achieving a 14.1% improvement in power coefficient
A 2018 paper constructing a conical spiral wind turbine from Fibonacci spiral blades, achieving 73.1% power extraction efficiency (with a theoretical ceiling of ~80%)
The Liam F1 as a rare example of a spiral turbine making it from lab research into a commercial product
Robert's own 3D-printed replication of the conical spiral turbine, including outdoor wind testing
Availability of open-access papers on spiral wind turbines via Google Scholar
materials: None mentioned.

items.t: 2209 Switched Flux Generators
topics: Limitations of conventional generators (universal motors, brushless DC motors) in harsh or high-frequency environments
The principle of magnetic reluctance — flux following the path of least reluctance
Switched flux / variable reluctance generation: redirecting a magnetic field without moving the magnet or coil
The Alexanderson Generator (1904) — historical use for high-power radio frequency generation
Modern applications: missile power supplies (e.g. SPARROW missile), guitar pickups
Advantages of switched flux generators: simplicity, robustness, no slip rings, no rotor coils, high-speed suitability
Turbines as an ideal drive mechanism for this generator type
materials: Steel (slotted rotor, demonstration piece)
Iron core (from a fan coil / microwave component)
Permanent magnets (adhered to the back of the coil)

items.t: 2208 The Great Fusion Breakthrough
topics: The NIF fusion ignition experiment (confirming a 2022 result)
Inertial confinement fusion (ICF) technique and how it works
The physics of nuclear fusion vs. nuclear fission
The three main approaches to fusion: magnetic confinement (tokamak), inertial electrostatic confinement, and gravitational (the Sun)
Cold fusion and alternative methods (muon-catalyzed fusion, etc.)
Safety and waste advantages of fusion over fission
Global fusion research landscape (US, France, UK)
materials: **Deuterium** — heavy hydrogen (1 proton + 1 neutron), one of the fusion fuel components
**Tritium** — heavier hydrogen isotope (1 proton + 2 neutrons), the other fusion fuel component; 220 micrograms used in the capsule
**Helium** — the fusion product when deuterium and tritium fuse
**Carbon** — material of the high-density fuel capsule (4.25 mg)
**Hydrogen** — discussed as the general basis for fusion fuels

items.t: 2207 Solar Power - We Have Been Doing It Wrong
topics: Wind vs. solar energy generation gap and why it exists
Concentrated solar power (CSP) — mirrors/reflectors heating water for steam turbines
Concentrated solar photovoltaics (CSV) — lenses/mirrors focusing light onto solar cells
Hyperstealth's lenticular lens + mirror concept for boosting solar output
Robert's live demonstration of the lens/mirror combination (~12% power increase)
Heat as the primary enemy of solar panel efficiency (0.3–0.4% loss per °C rise)
Netherlands Organisation for Applied Scientific Research (TNO) study on vertically mounted panels
Benefits of vertical mounting: no solar tracking, lower temperature, reduced degradation, cheaper frames
Bifacial solar panels and their efficiency advantage (11–23% over monofacial)
materials: **Lenticular (prism) lens film** — salvaged from an LCD display; used to focus/redirect light onto a solar panel
**Mirror** — bathroom mirror used as a reflector behind the lens; most effective reflector tested
**Aluminium foil** — tested as an alternative mirror surface; worked reasonably well
**Small solar panel** — purchased from Maplin; used as the test subject in the demonstration
**Heating coil** — from a kiln; used as the electrical load on the solar panel during testing

items.t: 2206 The RidgeBlade Wind Turbine And Understanding It
topics: Classification of wind turbines: horizontal axis (HAWT) vs. vertical axis (VAWT)
Savonius and Darrieus VAWT subtypes
Crossflow wind turbines: orientation, history, and how they work
Historical inventors: Paul Moutier (1891), Anthony Mitchell (1903), Donat Banki (1922), Fritz Ossberger
The Banki-Mitchell and Ossberger turbine lineage
Key design parameters for crossflow turbines (blade profile, blade number, blade angle, blade thickness ratio)
Power coefficient and limitations of crossflow turbines
Augmentation devices (guide nozzles, vanes, deflectors, cowlings)
The RidgeBlade as a crossflow turbine: advantages, cost structure, and urban suitability
materials: Lightweight materials (referenced generally as the construction material that keeps costs down and limits maximum wind speed tolerance)

items.t: 2205 Improving Your Solar With 3D Printing
topics: Fluorescent acrylic as a waveguide/light concentrator for solar cells
Fluorescent 3D printing filament (123-3D brand, yellow) as an optical fiber analogue
3D printing a right-angle prism (triangular prism) to act as a light guide over a solar cell
Total internal reflection in the printed prism
Over 10% improvement in solar cell output demonstrated live
Potential to improve results further by making printed filament transparent
materials: **Fluorescent acrylic panels** — used in prior video (2198) as waveguide/light concentrator reference
**Fluorescent yellow 3D printing filament** — sourced from 123-3D; found to be transparent and to act as a light guide
**Silicon solar cell** — used as the test photovoltaic device

items.t: Man In A Shed - Episode 4
topics: Coffee ground biochar as a concrete additive (up to 30% strength increase)
History and chemistry of Roman pozzolanic concrete
Wind energy harvesting via flutter, vortex shedding, galloping, and wind-induced vibration
3D-printed integral-hinge flutter energy harvesting device
Betavolt BV100 nuclear battery (nickel-63 + diamond semiconductor)
History of nuclear/radioisotope batteries from 1950s pacemakers to Bristol/Russian carbon-14 diamond batteries
DIY alpha-voltaic nuclear battery demonstration using americium-241 and a transistor
Historical unconventional engine fuels: acetylene, ethanol, coal dust, liquid air, ammonia, compressed air, gunpowder
Gunpowder chemistry, manufacture, and burn-rate tuning
Charlie Macklin's gunpowder-powered oscillating steam engine
3D-printed single-acting compressed air engine (minimal-part design)
materials: **Coffee grounds** — feedstock pyrolysed into biochar for concrete additive
**Biochar** — porous carbon-rich charcoal formed by pyrolysis; replaces 15% of sand by volume in concrete
**Activated carbon** — used as an oxygen-absorbing layer during the pyrolysis kiln process
**Pozzolan (pozzolanic ash)** — volcanic alumino-silicate used by Romans; reacts with calcium hydroxide to form insoluble hydrates
**Calcium hydroxide / lime** — reacts with pozzolan in the presence of water
**Calcium silicate hydrate / calcium aluminate hydrate** — reaction products that give Roman concrete its durability
**Nickel-63** — radioactive isotope (beta emitter) used in Betavolt BV100 nuclear battery; decays to harmless copper
**Single-crystal diamond** — semiconductor layers in the BV100 (10-micron layers sandwiching 2-micron Ni-63)
**Carbon-14** — radioactive graphite produced in nuclear reactors; used by Bristol University to grow radioactive diamond sheets for a battery
**Americium-241** — alpha-emitting radioisotope inside ionisation-type smoke alarms; used in the DIY nuclear battery demo; half-life ~432 years
**2N3055 transistor** — NPN silicon transistor used as the semiconductor junction in the DIY alpha-voltaic demo
**Potassium nitrate (saltpeter)** — oxidiser in gunpowder (75% by weight); food-grade, also sold as stump remover
**Sulfur** — fuel and ignition-temperature reducer in gunpowder (10% by weight)
**Softwood charcoal (pine)** — carbon fuel component of gunpowder (15% by weight); preferred over pure carbon
**Graphite** — tumbled onto corned gunpowder grains to prevent moisture ingress and static build-up
**Acetylene** — historically explored engine fuel; dangerous above 20 psi and forms explosive copper acetylide in brass/copper pipes
**Copper acetylide** — percussive explosive formed when acetylene contacts copper or brass
**Ethanol** — early external-combustion engine working fluid; expansion-engine variant predates internal combustion use
**Plutonium** — used in 1970s nuclear pacemaker batteries; ~88-year lifespan

items.t: 2204 Dirt Powered Fuel Cell That Runs Forever
topics: How microbial fuel cells work: microbes release electrons while breaking down organic matter in oxygen-deprived conditions
Historical context: MFCs first appeared in 1911 but were limited by low power and unreliability
Northwestern University's 10-year engineering project to find an optimal device geometry
Perpendicular anode/cathode design vs. traditional parallel design
3D printing as a fabrication method for the device housing
Application in agricultural IoT sensors (replacing solar/battery power in remote field deployments)
Open-access publication and public domain ethos — components available from hardware stores
Future plans for a fully biodegradable version
materials: **Carbon felt** — used for the electrodes; described as cheap, abundant, and innocuous
**Stainless steel** — used in some parts (water-jet cut in the prototype; replicable with a hacksaw and file)
**Soil** — the source of microbes and organic matter that fuel the cell
**Oxygen** — the electron acceptor at the cathode (air-exposed side)

items.t: 2203 The Harmony Wind Turbine - A Replication
topics: History of the Savonius turbine (Finnish, 1922) and its operating principle (differential drag)
Christopher Moore's Harmony turbine as a modern evolution of the Savonius design
The furling blade mechanism: blades open in low wind, close (furl) in high wind
How furling extends the operational wind speed range compared to conventional turbines
Robert's TinkerCAD design and 3D-printed replication of the Harmony turbine
The gear/cog mechanism and small control motor used to actuate the furling
Stacking multiple units into a helical configuration (as in Moore's commercial design)
Patent status and Penn State University involvement in research
materials: PLA or similar 3D printing filament (implied by 3D printing process, not explicitly named)
Glue (used to bond printed halves together)

items.t: 2202 The O-Wind - An Interesting VAWT (reworked and reposted)
topics: Background on the O-Wind turbine and its James Dyson Award win (2018)
Creators: Nicholas Aran and Yasin Nurani, Lancaster University
Patent analysis: US Patent 20210349 50 A1
Tetrahedral structure: how four triangular faces interlock
The Venturi effect and directional fin channels as the two core mechanisms
3D printed prototype built from the patent in TinkerCAD
Physical demonstration using a hair dryer to verify unidirectional rotation
Differences between the patent design and the forthcoming commercial product
Skepticism about a "700× better than solar" claim circulating in media
materials: Steel bar (8 mm diameter) — used as the axis of rotation in the prototype
Skate bearings (608 type, 22 mm × 8 mm × 7 mm) — mounted in the foot of the prototype for low-friction rotation

items.t: 2160 War Beasts
topics: General history of animals in warfare (horses, dogs, pigeons, elephants, dolphins, sea lions)
Soviet anti-tank dogs (1930s–1943): training, deployment, and catastrophic failure
Japanese "demolition wolves": similar concept targeting bunkers, terminated December 1943
American bat bombs (Project X-Ray, 1943–1944): incendiary bats targeting Japanese cities
Historical animal bomb carriers (oxen, donkeys, mules, pigs, monkeys — including pitch-and-fire tactics)
Project Pigeon / Project Orcon: B.F. Skinner's pigeon-guided bombs
Revival of pigeon use by the US Coast Guard (1979) for sea search-and-rescue
Ethical debates and practical limitations of using animals in warfare
materials: Explosives (strapped to dogs and other animals)
Incendiary devices (miniature waistcoat-mounted timers worn by bats)
Pitch (used to coat animals set alight as incendiary weapons)

items.t: 2201 Waste Plastic To Spider Silk
topics: Destructive distillation of HDPE plastic (bottles and bags) into a waxy fuel
Limitations and criticisms of plastic-to-fuel as a recycling strategy
Catalytic distillation using strontium titanium trioxide to reduce energy input
Bacterial fermentation of the waxy HDPE byproduct into spider silk protein
Properties and applications of spider silk (strength, weight, biodegradability)
Comparison of fermentation to familiar processes (bread, cheese, beer, penicillin)
materials: **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)** — source plastic (bottles, bags), used as feedstock
**Strontium titanium trioxide (SrTiO₃)** — catalyst used to lower distillation temperature
**Waxy hydrocarbon product** — solid byproduct of HDPE destructive distillation, acts as bacterial food source
**Spider silk / golden silk** — the target bioplastic protein output
**Kevlar** — referenced as a strength comparison for spider silk

items.t: 2200 Wind Turbines - The Future Is Vertical
topics: History of VAWTs from 9th-century Persia through the Darrieus designs of the 1920s
Catalogue of catastrophic VAWT failures in the 1980s (Prince Edward Island, San Gorgonio Pass, England, Carmarthen Bay)
Economic limitations of HAWTs: current cost ~$122/MWh, target <$50/MWh to compete with fossil fuels (~$38/MWh)
Physical limits of HAWT technology: material constraints, 4.5% annual efficiency degradation, 80% failure probability after 8 years, high maintenance costs
The C2 Wind Turbine (Twirl): S1 prototype (2015, 30 kW) and current 1 MW prototype for a 5-year trial
Research finding that paired/clustered VAWTs benefit from each other's turbulence, yielding ~15% efficiency improvement
The paradigm shift this combination of advances could represent
materials: Steel (~700 tons per 6 MW HAWT plant)
Concrete (~5,000 tons per 6 MW HAWT plant)
Fiberglass (VAWT blade material, cited in the 1986 Carmarthen Bay failure)

items.t: Man In A Shed - Episode 3 - Fluorescent Solar
topics: Building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) vs. centralised solar farms
Light guides (from LCD screens and LED lighting panels) as concentrating PV (CPV) systems
Fluorescent dyes as spectrum converters for solar cells
Passive solar tracking using prism/triangle shapes coated in fluorescent material
University of Rochester research on light guide CPV (reporting ~50% efficiency improvement)
Cost breakdown of solar installations (panels ~20%, installation ~35% of total cost)
materials: **Acrylic light guide panels** — extracted from LCD screens and LED office lighting panels; used to concentrate light onto solar cells
**Fluorescent highlighter ink** — water-soluble dye extracted from highlighter pens; used as the fluorescent absorber
**Gelatin** — used to suspend the fluorescent dye in a jelly/gel medium
**Deionised water** — solvent for extracting highlighter dye
**Cellulose acetate** — the absorbent felt material inside highlighter pens
**Fluorescent acrylic sheets** — commercial alternative to DIY jelly (~£10 each)
**Fluorescent safety shirt (yellow dye)** — used as an initial test of fluorescence boosting solar output
**Perovskites** — mentioned as thin-film solar material for building integration (not used experimentally)
**Multi-junction solar cells / GaAs solar cells** — mentioned as high-efficiency (~40–45%) options

items.t: 2199 Heating And Cooling Without Power - Wax Motors And Paint
topics: The scale of domestic energy use for heating and cooling (~55%)
Wax motors (linear actuators): how they work and where they are found
Vantablack and competing super-black coatings as solar absorbers
Purdue University's radiative cooling "whitest white" paint
The UC Santa Barbara switchable roofing tile — design, operation, and results
Potential longevity of wax-motor-based roof tiles (decades)
Accessibility of the concept for DIY experimentation
materials: **Wax motor** — copper cup filled with wax; expands 5–20% on melting to drive a linear actuator
**Vantablack** — super-black coating absorbing 99.96% of UV, visible, and infrared light; used as the heat-absorbing side of the tile
**Radiative cooling white paint** — developed by Purdue University; reflects heat back to space; used as the heat-rejecting side of the tile
**Copper** — material of the wax motor cup

items.t: 2198 The Spherical Solar Cell
topics: The Beta.ey spherical solar concentrator by André Brøssel / company Rawlemon
How the glass sphere acts as a ball lens to concentrate light onto a small PV cell
Dual-axis solar tracking enabled by the small PV cell size
Hybrid thermal-electric concept combining PV with a Stirling engine
The moonlight harvesting claim
Hands-on demonstration with a bullseye lens from a car headlight
Fluorescent/luminescent waveguides as a method for light concentration and wavelength conversion
General problems with concentrated photovoltaic (CPV) systems: heat, maintenance, and optical losses
materials: **Glass** — large sphere used as a ball lens in the Beta.ey device
**Acrylic (PMMA)** — fluorescent-dye-filled panel used as a luminescent solar concentrator / waveguide
**Fluorescent material/dye** — embedded in the acrylic to absorb and re-emit light at a useful wavelength for the solar cell
**Lithium battery** — found inside the disassembled cheap fence solar light

items.t: 2197 The Infinite CVT And Wind Turbine Control
topics: Planetary gear system fundamentals (sun, planet, ring gears, planet carrier)
Gear ratio calculations for planetary systems
Modified ring gear with external teeth enabling dual-input drive
Continuously variable transmission (CVT) using electronic motor control
Application of the CVT concept to wind turbine speed regulation
Self-regulating mechanical feedback loop using an idler gear
Comparison to governor mechanisms; note that the system already exists in the Toyota Prius
materials: 3D-printed plastic gear components (designed in Tinkercad)
Skate bearings (22 mm × 7 mm × 8 mm)
8 mm steel bar (axle)
Washers
Glue (for idler gear assembly)

items.t: 2196 The Spherical Coulisse Power Transfer Mechanism
topics: Overview of the four main types of power transmission: electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, and mechanical
The six types of mechanical power transmission: shaft couplings, chain drives, gear drives, belt drives, power screws/lead screws, and the coulisse mechanism
The coulisse (French for "slide") mechanism in both linear and spherical forms
The Hobson linkage as a prior example of a coulisse drive, transmitting rotation through 90° with both shafts turning in the same direction
The spherical coulisse drive: design origin (th1146), redesign in Tinkercad, and 3D printing for demonstration
Operating characteristics: 2:1 speed ratio (two input turns = one output turn), doubled output torque, non-back-drivable
Potential applications: safety linkages, tools, trip hammers, reciprocating saws, differentials
materials: 10 mm ball bearing (recommended over the printed ball included in the file)
3D-printed PLA parts (seven components total, clip-together assembly)
Adhesive/glue (small amount to secure the interfacing gear)

items.t: 2195 Bizarre Gears
topics: Fundamental principles of gears: three motion types (rotation, translation/sliding, screw) and three axle arrangements (parallel/in-plane, angled/bevel, skewed/worm)
Geneva mechanism — used historically in film cameras for precise stop-and-go motion
Bevel gears — used in car differentials; axles meet at an angle (commonly 90°)
Worm gears — skewed axle arrangement; can provide speed differentials
Mangle gear — a rack-and-pinion variant for back-and-forth translation
Cube gear and Heart gear — by Emmert Lish; eight bevel gears with axles meeting at the cube's centre
Brin gear — follows an octahedron geometry rather than a cube; bevel gears left untruncated
Fibonacci/nautilus (non-circular) gears — one gear speeds up and slows down relative to the other during rotation
Oval gears — used in mechanical flow meters; interlocking creates sealed pockets to measure liquid volume
Half-sphere gears — by Oscar van Deventer; can fold 180° like a hinge while remaining interlocked
Vennin gear (ACROBAT — Active Ball Joint Mechanism with Three Degrees of Freedom) — spherical gear developed at Yamagata University; used in robotics
Mangle rack — a pin-based mechanism used by the akiyuki Brick channel in Lego models
Borromean Rings gear mechanism — three interlocking identical gears in a rectangular arrangement that can still move together
Henry Segerman's interlocking donut gear system — three inseparable torus shapes driven by a single corkscrew spindle

items.t: 2194 Hydrogen On Demand - Improvements And A Possible Conspiracy
topics: History and properties of aluminium, including its reactivity and oxide passivation layer
Aluminium reacting with water (via alkali stripping of the oxide coat) to generate hydrogen, electricity, and heat
Cost comparison: aluminium-fuelled hydrogen vs. conventional petrol for a 350-mile trip
Internal combustion engine vs. fuel cell efficiency for hydrogen use
The gallium–aluminium interaction as a hydrogen-on-demand system (Purdue University 2007, Santa Cruz 2022 with nanoparticles)
The François Cornish patent (tested by BMW in 1981) using high-voltage sparking of aluminium wire in water
Recent research into alternative activating alloys (indium, tin, bismuth) and nitrogen activation
The prosaic reality of why most patents never reach market (counter to conspiracy theories)
materials: **Aluminium** — primary fuel/reactant for hydrogen generation
**Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃)** — passive protective coating on aluminium; byproduct of the reaction, recyclable back to refineries
**Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** — strips aluminium oxide to expose bare metal
**Potassium hydroxide (KOH)** — used in the live demonstration as the electrolyte/stripping solution
**Gallium** — forms a liquid alloy with aluminium to prevent oxide reformation; reusable
**Mercury** — historically used (by H. C. Ørsted) to activate aluminium; not recommended today
**Indium** — mentioned as an alternative alloying element to gallium
**Tin** — mentioned as an alternative alloying element
**Bismuth** — mentioned as an alternative alloying element
**Nitrogen** — used in a recent (2024) paper to activate aluminium, showing improvement over prior systems
**Water** — co-reactant consumed alongside aluminium to produce hydrogen

items.t: 2193 Energy Farms - From Plant Power To Power Plant
topics: Plants as electrical generators — an underappreciated capability
Microbial fuel cells using root-zone microorganism activity (including moss walls and small demonstration devices like a clock and coffee table)
Direct photosynthesis harvesting: using platinum and iron electrodes inserted into leaf cells to create living solar cells
Transpiration-based power generation: ion movement through evaporating water as an electricity source
A fabric-based transpiration device used to construct a power-generating tent capable of charging a mobile phone
materials: **Platinum electrodes** — used to access photosynthetic current within leaf cells
**Iron electrodes** — paired with platinum in the living solar cell experiment
**Copper and zinc electrodes** — mentioned as an earlier (less effective) approach, likened to a "glorified lemon battery"
**Carbon electrodes** — used in the transpiration fabric device
**Hydrogen gas** — a by-product collectible at the cathode during the leaf photosynthesis experiment
**Water** — the working fluid in both photosynthesis and transpiration electricity generation
***Cuscuta* sp. (likely *Cuscuta lanii*)** — the plant specimen used in the 2022 living solar cell study

items.t: 2192 MIT Breakthrough On Making All The Fresh Water We Need
topics: Global freshwater scarcity: only ~1.2% of Earth's water is accessible fresh water
The natural water cycle as the basis for desalination thinking
History and limitations of simple solar stills
The salt-clogging problem in solar evaporation systems
MIT's thermohaline circulation-based solar still
DIY replication using Oasis foam and activated carbon felt
Cost comparison: MIT system vs. household tap water
materials: **Seawater / salt water** — the input feed for desalination
**Oasis foam** — a floral/craft foam used for its capillary wicking properties, described as mimicking plant cell structure
**Activated carbon felt** — black felt used to absorb solar radiation and accelerate surface evaporation
**Brine** — the concentrated salt byproduct discharged from the MIT system

items.t: Man In A Shed - Episode 2
topics: Three strategies for controlling heat in appliances: passive (Ohm's Law), bimetallic switching, and PID controllers
Ohm's Law (V = IR) and resistance heating; comparison of copper vs. nichrome wire
The WWII-era GM-44 hand-crank field generator and Robert's exercise-bike remake
Converting an exercise-bike flywheel into a direct generator using neodymium magnets and a salvaged microwave fan coil
Microwave kilns: history, construction, and DIY build using a flower pot and graphite paint
The Oxford Electric Bell (dry pile battery) — history, operation, and debate over electrostatic vs. chemical mechanism
Building a DIY Zamboni-style dry pile from aluminium foil and conductive ink
Converting a petrol generator to run on propane/LPG (and biopropane from algae/bacteria)
Tensegrity structures ("floating tables") and their applications in architecture, wind turbines, and energy harvesting
Water motors and the Turgo turbine; 3D-printed Turgo turbine design and first test
materials: **Nichrome wire** — high-resistance alloy used in heating elements; nickel, chromium, and iron alloy
**Copper wire** — low-resistance conductor used for comparison
**Neodymium magnets** — 1 cm × 30 cm × 3 mm, used on flywheel to create a generator
**Super glue (cyanoacrylate)** — used to bond neodymium magnets to the steel flywheel
**Fiberglass resin** — used to mount the microwave fan coil to the flywheel
**Graphite / graphene-based conductive paint** (specifically Rust-ins G-Shield mentioned as an example) — used as the susceptor coating inside the microwave kiln
**Ceramic fibre blanket** — insulation wrapped around the DIY kiln (~100 mm thick)
**Clay flower pot** — base body of the cheap DIY microwave kiln (withstands ~500–600 °C)
**Stoneware** — recommended kiln body material (withstands ~800 °C)
**Porcelain** — preferred kiln body material (withstands ~1200 °C)
**Alumina / aluminium oxide brick** — ideal kiln body material, equivalent to commercial kilns
**Zinc foil** — traditional Zamboni pile layer material
**Manganese dioxide** — traditional Zamboni pile electrode material; releases oxygen on heating
**Aluminium (roofing) foil** — used as substitute for zinc foil in the DIY dry pile
**Conductive ink (non-waterproof)** — used as a substitute carbon layer in the DIY dry pile
**Water glass (sodium silicate)** — mentioned as a permanent binder for graphite susceptor paint
**Propane / butane / LPG** — fuels used in generator conversion; discussed as "drop-in" alternatives to petrol
**Biopropane** — propane derived from biomass, algae, or bacteria (e.g., *E. coli*-produced propane discussed)
**Ethanol** — noted as a component of modern petrol blends causing storage separation issues
**Lead sulfide / lead telluride** — mentioned in the context of WWII infrared telescope image converter tubes powered by dry piles
**Sulfur** — noted as the outer sealing layer of Oxford Bell dry piles
**Tin foil discs / zinc-sulfur-impregnated paper / manganese dioxide** — layers in Zamboni-type dry piles
**608 bearings** — standard skateboard bearings used in the 3D-printed Turgo turbine
**8 mm steel rod** — axle for the turbine
**D-shaped draft excluder foam** — used as a water seal on the turbine housing

items.t: 2191 Self Charging Solar Battery
topics: History and efficiency of solar cells (copper oxide, silicon, perovskites, tandem cells)
The Shockley-Queisser efficiency limit (~33.7%)
The concept of a solar cell that is also its own battery
Prussian Blue as a photoelectrochemical energy storage material
Hands-on synthesis of Prussian Blue on filter paper
Construction and demonstration of a simple Prussian Blue solar battery cell
Electrochromic windows as a related technology
materials: **Copper oxide** — early, low-efficiency solar cell material (~4%)
**Silicon** — modern solar cells with 18–22% efficiency
**Perovskites** — emerging solar material at ~25% efficiency, stability issues
**Prussian Blue (iron(III) hexacyanoferrate)** — the core electrochromic energy storage material
**Potassium ferrocyanide (K4)** — one synthesis route for Prussian Blue, paired with an iron(III) salt
**Iron(III) chloride** — iron source for the K4 synthesis route
**Potassium ferricyanide (K3)** — the synthesis route used in the video
**Iron(II) sulfate** — iron source for the K3 route; noted as a fertilizer and cheaper option
**Potassium chloride (3M solution)** — used as the electrolyte
**Aluminium** — used as one electrode, assists oxidation of Prussian Blue to Prussian White
**Stainless steel mesh** — used as the semi-transparent counter electrode
**Coffee filter paper** — substrate for depositing Prussian Blue

items.t: 2190 An Unusual Air Engine
topics: History of alternative engines (steam, compressed air, gunpowder, liquid air, ammonia)
Advantages and disadvantages of compressed air as a power source
3D-printed compressed air engine build (remixed from a design by "newly")
Assembly walkthrough of the single-acting engine
Converting steam and internal combustion engines to run on compressed air
Off-the-shelf air engine demonstration (eBay, ~£20)
materials: Compressed air (working fluid)
M8 nuts (used as flywheel ballast weights)
Glue (for assembly of piston hook and feed spigot)
Teac cut / cutting compound (mentioned as an option to ease tight-fitting parts)

items.t: 2189 O-Wind And A Way To Assess New Wind Turbine Designs
topics: Robert's personal enthusiasm for DIY wind generation
The "wild west" problem of unverified wind turbine claims in media
Paul Gipe's four-point framework for assessing wind turbine credibility
Application of the framework to the O-Wind turbine specifically
The importance of distinguishing genuine innovation from hype or carnival-style promotion
The distinction between a turbine spinning in wind vs. actually generating electrical energy efficiently

items.t: Man In A Shed - Episode 1 - A Pilot Re-uploaded
topics: Pliosaur anatomy and locomotion — the four-flipper figure-of-eight swimming motion and its 50% efficiency/thrust gains (Dr Luke Muscutt, University of Southampton)
Biomimicry: how pliosaur flipper vortex mechanics parallel goose-formation aerodynamics and inform dual-rotor wind turbine design
OWI Wind Turbine — James Dyson Award 2018 winner, spherical/tetrahedral design, Venturi-effect wind capture from any direction; Robert builds and tests a 3D-printed version from the US patent
Repurposing waste: abandoned shopping trolleys converted into wheelchairs (Thailand case study); China's surplus bicycle-share fleet as mobility resource
3D scanning with the Creality Ferret Pro: scanning the Gandhi statue in Parliament Square and other public sculptures
Scan the World — MyMiniFactory's open-access 3D scan archive of global cultural artifacts
Art UK — UK's digital catalog of publicly owned sculptures and paintings
Carpon pile — a "forever battery" running since 1950 in Romania; Robert replicates a Type 3 cell from French patent FR 57787, tests temperature dependence of output voltage

Gravity energy storage: Archimedes screw worm gear, spiral-drop ratchet generator, humming-top ratchet mechanism, and a student-built portable gravity-powered emergency generator from the Technological University of the Philippines
materials: **Graphite foil** — used as electrodes in the Carpon pile replication
**Sodium carbonate (washing soda)** — electrolyte in the Type 3 Carpon cell
**Copper chloride solution** — electrolyte in a second replication attempt (fish tank setup)
**Aluminium chloride** — used in the heated electrolyte test to demonstrate temperature-voltage dependence
**Gold and platinum electrodes** — used in the original 1950 Carpon pile (National Technical Museum, Romania)
**Sulfuric acid** — electrolyte in the original Carpon cell

items.t: 2188 A Gunpowder Engine
topics: The principle of engine operation via expansion of a working fluid
Internal vs. external combustion engines, and compressed-air engines
Historical alternative fuel engines (ethanol, acetylene/acetylene, coal dust)
History of gunpowder engines: Christopher Huygens, George Cayley, Thomas Paine designs
MythBusters Season 4, Episode 25 test of gunpowder engines (marked "busted")
Charlie Macklin's successful oscillating steam engine powered by homemade gunpowder
Why store-bought gunpowder burns too fast for use in an engine
History of gunpowder (first recorded use 904 AD, China; formula standardized ~1758)
Gunpowder chemistry: the roles of sulfur, carbon (charcoal), and potassium nitrate
Mixing, milling, corning, and graphite-coating processes used in gunpowder manufacture
How grain size and composition affect burn rate, and why this matters for different applications
materials: **Potassium nitrate (saltpeter/KNO₃)** — oxidizer, 75% by weight; available as food-grade curing salt or stump remover
**Carbon/charcoal** — fuel, 15% by weight; specifically softwood charcoal (pine) recommended over pure carbon
**Sulfur** — fuel and ignition-temperature reducer, 10% by weight
**Ethanol** — discussed as a historical expansion-engine fuel; exhaust was flammable vapor
**Acetylene** — discussed as a dangerous alternative engine fuel; hazardous above 20 psi, forms explosive copper acetylide in copper/brass pipes
**Coal dust** — historical powdered-fuel engine feedstock
**Alcohol** — used wet-milling gunpowder ingredients
**Graphite** — used to coat powder grains to prevent moisture absorption and static buildup
**Limestone / lead** — non-sparking milling materials historically used in powder mills
**Brown powder** — slow-burning variant made with under-charred (brown) charcoal or charred straw

items.t: 2187 - The 50 Year Nuclear Battery From China And How To Make Your Own Version
topics: Betavolt's BV100 battery: specs, operating principle, and production timeline

materials: **Nickel-63** — radioactive isotope used in Betavolt's BV100 as the beta particle source
**Diamond (single-crystal, CVD)** — semiconductor material sandwiching the nickel-63 layer; 10-micron layers
**Carbon-14** — produced in nuclear reactor graphite rods when carbon-12/13 absorbs fast neutrons; used in Bristol's diamond battery
**Graphite (reactor-grade)** — decommissioned nuclear reactor moderator rods, source material for radioactive diamond
**Americium-241** — alpha-emitting isotope found inside ionization-type smoke alarms; half-life ~432 years
**Plutonium** — mentioned as the isotope used in 1970s nuclear pacemakers
**Copper** — the harmless decay product of nickel-63

**Pozzolana / pozzolanic ash** — volcanic alumino-silicate ash used by Romans; reacts with lime and water to form binding compounds

items.t: 2177 Gravity Batteries - Cheap, Easy, Robust And Awesome For Off Grid

**Copper sulfate (CuSO₄)** — cathodic electrolyte in the primary cell; placed at the bottom

**Sodium bromide (NaBr)** — assistant salt recommended at 1 mol alongside 5 mol ZnBr₂ to maintain conductivity
**Bromine (Br₂)** — forms at the carbon electrode during charging; denser than ZnBr₂ solution, sinks to bottom
**Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** — mentioned as an alternative electrolyte additive (not used here due to safety)
**Machine/motor oil** — poured on top as a seal to prevent evaporation, salt creep, and bromine off-gassing

items.t: 2130 Controlling Heat

items.t: 043 Damp Paper - The Future Of Energy

**Graphene oxide (GO)** — key high-performance MEG material; brown-black viscous liquid

items.t: The Dangers Of Lead

items.t: 042 The Earth Battery
topics: Historical background: Galvani, Volta, the voltaic pile, Lord Kelvin's 1836 seawater battery proposal, and 19th–20th century research into buried electrodes
The generator vs. battery debate: galvanic/corrosion action, telluric earth currents (flowing pole-to-pole and toward the Sun), and exoelectrogenic bacteria as competing or complementary explanations
Effect of electrode orientation (north–south alignment affects current, not voltage), soil moisture, surface area, and electrode material choice
Practical outdoor demonstration: measuring voltage and current from various electrode pairs using a multimeter and dummy load
The series-connection problem: earth-buried cells normally cannot be stacked in series because the soil shorts them together
Plastic pipe isolation technique: driving short ABS pipe sections into the ground before inserting electrodes allows successful series stacking
Exoelectrogenic bacteria: their electrically conductive protein filaments, electron-transport biology (ATP/ADP cycle), and roles as electricity producers, consumers, and biological wires
Potential applications: remote lighting, fuel cells, water purification, NASA interest in off-world energy generation
materials: **Carbon rods** — salvaged from zinc-carbon batteries; used as cathode in most pairings; produced the highest voltages
**Zinc** — used as anode in the first test pairing
**Brass** — used as a cathode proxy for copper in early tests
**Copper** — briefly tested against carbon
**Aluminium** — tested against carbon; produced ~1 V
**Magnesium** — tested against carbon; produced ~2 V (highest voltage recorded)
**Lead** — tested as anode against brass and carbon; noted as effective but flagged as a safety concern
**Stainless steel** — tested against carbon; produced ~0.5 V; noted as unlikely to be corrosion-dependent
**ABS plastic pipe** (25 mm diameter, cut to 10 cm sections) — used to isolate individual cells in the ground
**Salt solutions** — mentioned from a patent as a soil treatment to improve conductivity in isolated pits

topics: Why neodymium magnets are essential for electric vehicles and wind turbines (Lenz's law)
The misnomer of "rare earth": neodymium is abundant but difficult and damaging to extract
China's dominance (~81%) of neodymium supply and associated geopolitical concerns
The difference between paramagnetic and ferromagnetic materials
How magnetism is also a property of physical shape/structure, not just chemistry
Tetrataenite: a nickel-iron mineral found naturally only in meteorites
The 2022 Cambridge University paper showing tetrataenite can be synthesized by adding phosphorus to a nickel-iron alloy
materials: **Neodymium (Nd)** — rare earth element, ~30% of neodymium magnets; paramagnetic, not intrinsically ferromagnetic
**Iron (Fe)** — majority constituent of neodymium magnets; ferromagnetic above 770°C (Curie point)
**Boron (B)** — minor constituent of NdFeB magnets
**Nickel (Ni)** — ferromagnetic material; constituent of tetrataenite
**Phosphorus (P)** — the key additive that enables tetrataenite to form in seconds during casting
**Copper carbonate (malachite)** — used as an analogy to illustrate easy metal extraction vs. rare earth difficulty
**Carbon / charcoal** — reducing agent in the copper smelting analogy
**Carbon monoxide** — the reactive species in the copper reduction process

items.t: 2121 Thrust From Capacitors And How To Make A Speaker From A Pencil And A Crisp Packet
topics: Definition and distinction between two types of asymmetric capacitors (supercapacitor sense vs. geometry sense)
Ion wind lifters vs. pure electrostatic field thrust
Asymmetric electrostatic fields as a source of propulsion
NASA's interest in field-based thrust for space travel
Electrostatic speakers as a practical application
DIY demonstration with household materials
materials: Aluminium (used as capacitor plate material)
Manganese dioxide (mentioned as an example material in asymmetric supercapacitors)
Coffee can (large metal plate for the asymmetric capacitor demo)
9B graphite pencil (used to draw a capacitor structure on paper)
Crisp packet / aluminized Mylar foil (aluminium layer sandwiched between two plastic films, used as a floating plate)
Window film (used as an alternative dielectric/plate in a second demo)

items.t: A Few Words On Quantum Magnetics And The PMMG4Hybrid Battery
topics: Overview of the PMMG4 Hybrid Silicon Carbide Nano Battery and its $4,000 price point
Robert's lack of direct experience with the device and his limitations in evaluating it
Cost comparison: buying conventional batteries vs. this product
Comparison to a "glorified Bini SSG" — a device he estimates could be replicated for ~$200
Absence of independent test data or white papers on the product
Skepticism toward buzzword-heavy marketing ("Nano," "free energy," "quantum")
Historical analogy to radium being marketed as a wonder ingredient in the 1950s
Offer to test the device if the manufacturer sends one
Mention of his own magnesium battery research as a credible alternative
materials: **Silicon carbide** — part of the product's claimed composition
**Magnesium** — referenced as a material Robert has used in his own battery experiments (1 cm² slice reportedly ran a motor for ~4 days)
**Graphene** — mentioned as a material the manufacturer separately sells in a "graphene supercapacitor" product

items.t: 2151 Chemistry For Everyone - Mechanochemical Synthesis of Copper Inks And Nanoparticals
topics: What mechanochemical synthesis is and why it matters (green chemistry, solvent-free, low temperature)
How mechanical force acts as a catalyst by breaking kinetic barriers and promoting molecular rearrangement
Applications across pharmaceuticals, nanomaterials, polymers, ceramics, MOFs, and composites
Practical synthesis of copper formate and copper nanoparticles using a consumer spice grinder
Uses of the resulting material: copper plating on steel, conductive organometallic inks for inkjet printing, copper nanoparticle/nanorod formation in graphite/carbon matrices
Safety considerations for handling 85% formic acid
materials: **Copper(I) oxide (Cu₂O)** — red powder; the copper source, reduced by formic acid under mechanochemical conditions
**Copper(II) oxide (CuO)** — black powder; mentioned for comparison but not used in this experiment
**Formic acid (85%)** — powerful lachrymator; reacts with copper oxide to form copper formate and copper particles
**Copper formate** — blue product of the reaction; thermally decomposes at ~200°C to yield copper, CO₂, and water
**Copper nanoparticles** — black; produced by thermal decomposition of copper formate
**Copper particles (sub-micron, salmon pink)** — larger than true nanoparticles; useful for painted/brushed copper traces
**Monoethanolamine / triethanolamine** — added to copper formate to form a sticky blue copper complex used for coating
**Graphite / carbon** — mixed with copper formate and heated to form copper nanoparticles or nanorods within the carbon matrix
**Granite** — material of the spice grinder stones; inert grinding medium

items.t: Open Mindedness
topics: Robert's background: poverty, crime, and turning to education in his mid-teens
The influence of his father getting a degree as a model for self-improvement
The concept of personal responsibility: your life's *view* is within your control
The "trauma" of realising you are not the centre of the world
Three principles of open-mindedness: (1) you are not the centre of the world, (2) you are your own responsibility, (3) you can only control yourself
Using his perpetual motion video as a case study in open vs. closed thinking
Civility in disagreement as a prerequisite for open-minded discourse
Praising commenter Kimberly Peacock as an example of open-minded enquiry

items.t: 2129 The Kaleidoscope - A Forgotten Craze in Victorian England.
topics: History of mirror-reflection devices from Giambattista della Porta (1500s) through Athanasius Kircher (1646) to David Brewster (1815)
Brewster's invention, patent, and partnership with manufacturer Philip Carpenter
The Victorian kaleidoscope craze (~200,000 units sold almost immediately)
Connection between kaleidoscope symmetry and fractal self-similarity
Variations of the kaleidoscope: telescopic, microscopic, reflective, projecting, sculptural, and architectural
Step-by-step DIY "collidoscope" build from breadboards
materials: **Breadboard (wood)** — two boards (one dark, one light) used as the main structural material
**Acrylic mirror tiles** — cut into strips to form the internal mirror triangle; noted as easier to cut than glass
**Marbles (25 mm)** — placed at the bottom of the scope to create the kaleidoscopic object

items.t: On Risk
topics: The distinction between "real risk" and "formalized risk"
The three pillars of managing real risk: acquaintance, knowledge, and experience
Examples of real risk across professions: chemistry labs, cleaning Big Ben, firefighting, crossing the road
UK workplace accident statistics comparing chemical injuries (~600) to injuries from walking into static objects (~135,000)
Critique of the Health and Safety Executive's tendency to layer rules without removing outdated ones
Blame culture, ambulance-chasing lawyers, and the erosion of personal responsibility
The chimpanzee/ladder/bananas anecdote as a metaphor for unquestioned corporate procedures
What a genuinely good health and safety approach should look like
materials: **Sulfuric acid** — cited as a canonical example of a dangerous laboratory chemical that all lab workers must know to avoid contact with

items.t: On Gurus
topics: The "guru" phenomenon on internet forums
The difference between genuine knowledge-sharing and control-seeking behaviour
Why technical jargon can be exclusionary
Robert's moderation philosophy for his channel
The value of comments as genuine feedback and learning for him

items.t: 2143 Generator Secrets
topics: Internal anatomy of a brushed DC motor (commutator segments, coils, pole pieces, permanent magnets)
Converting a motor into a generator by spinning the magnet array past fixed coils
Frames of reference: spinning the outer magnet case vs. spinning the rotor are equivalent
Using 46 magnets in a north-south alternating ring as a "gearing" effect (23× pole-pair equivalent)
Coreless vs. cored coils: trade-off between output power and cogging
Single-coil output measurement (~28 mA, ~1.2 W) under resistive load (soldering iron)
Scaling to multiple coils around the flywheel
Speed as the dominant factor in generator output (power ∝ voltage², voltage ∝ speed)
materials: Hand blender motor (brushed DC, ~£5)
46 permanent magnets arranged in a north-south alternating ring
Turntable motor synchronous coils (coreless, many turns of thin wire)
Single open-core (coreless) test coil
Soldering iron used as a resistive load

items.t: 040 The Power Of Whales - Tubercles On Everything
topics: History and science of whale tubercles: marine biologist Frank Fish and engineer Philip Watts' research showing 15–20% improvement in lift and drag reduction
Patent implications: patents cover commercial use only, leaving private/educational use open
3D printing and testing tubercle-covered blades vs. smooth blades side-by-side
Introduction to **SculptGL** as an intuitive, browser-based 3D sculpting tool for cleaning up scanned STL files
Fan blade testing: toroidal (petal) fan blade with and without tubercles, measuring airspeed and amp draw
Water turbine demonstration using a garden hose on a tubercle blade mounted to a PC fan generator
Vertical-axis wind turbines (VAWTs): design types (Darrieus, Savonius, helical), advantages/disadvantages, and tubercle integration
VAWT RPM comparison: 100 RPM without tubercles vs. 149 RPM with tubercles
Biomimicry as a design philosophy
materials: **PLA/3D-printed filament** — used to print all blade prototypes
**Steel rod** — axle for bearing mounts
**Skateboard bearings** (22mm × 8mm × 7mm) — used in VAWT cradle assembly
**Cyanoacrylate (superglue)** — for assembling VAWT components
**Plaster of Paris** — mentioned as an option for mold-making from clay models
**Clay** (natural, dug from a hill) — used to sculpt tubercle models for scanning
**Plasticine / Milliput** — mentioned as alternative modeling materials
**Reflective aluminium strip** — used as tachometer target on VAWT rotor
**3V battery pack** — power source for fan blade motor tests

items.t: 2142 Thermopile And MHD - Generating From Rocket Stoves
topics: Comparison of the thermopile (MIM diode-based) and MHD generator side by side
Explanation of how the thermopile uses heat differential across a MIM diode to rectify electron flow
Explanation of how the MHD generator uses a conductive, moving fluid (flame) through a magnetic field — analogous to a conventional wire-wound generator
Why coils would not work in the MHD device (electrode separation is required, not induction)
Magnet selection and Curie temperature considerations for the MHD setup
Industrial-scale MHD generation and the technique of using generated current to power electromagnet coils
Limitations of Peltier devices in high-temperature rocket stove contexts
materials: **Copper** — used for thermopile construction and as electrode pickups in the MHD generator (noted as not ideal for electrodes)
**Tungsten** — suggested as a better electrode material for the MHD generator
**Nichrome** — also suggested as a better electrode material
**Potassium ions** — seeded into the flame to increase its electrical conductivity
**Ceramic (ferrite) magnets** — permanent magnets used in the desktop MHD model; Curie temperature ~450°C
**Samarium cobalt magnets** — noted as higher-temperature alternative; Curie temperature ~800–850°C
**Neodymium magnets** — noted as lower Curie temperature (~370°C), less suitable for high-heat applications
**Peltier devices** — discussed as an alternative but dismissed due to low solder melting points (~250°C) and high cost/low efficiency of high-temperature variants

items.t: On Belief
topics: The nature of belief and why it cannot be proven true
Scientific beliefs vs. other beliefs (e.g., Earth orbiting the Sun) as equally unprovable outside their own frameworks
Consensus as a social phenomenon, not a measure of truth
The philosophical split between idealism and realism
Utility as one (imperfect) measure of a belief system's value
Accepting your belief system without needing to justify or defend it

items.t: Making The World A Better Place
topics: Freely sharing technical solutions (specifically a conductive ink formulation) with someone who sought to profit from it
The Gandhi quote: "be the thing you want the world to be"
Personal philosophy of making small, incremental positive contributions
Why Robert shares knowledge publicly on the internet and helps people directly
The distinction between controlling one's own behaviour vs. trying to change others
materials: Conductive ink (mentioned as the subject of the technical query; no specific formulation or chemicals disclosed)

items.t: 2140 DIY Heaters
topics: Disassembly of a commercial fan heater to reveal its internal components
Disassembly of a toaster to compare heating elements and circuit design
How bimetallic strip thermostats and thermal cutout switches work
Using toaster heating elements as a low-voltage hand warmer
Safety considerations for low-voltage DC heaters vs. mains-powered devices
Graphene-based conductive paint (Elro Smog Paint) as a heater material

**Nichrome (NiCr) wire** — the resistive heating element in both the fan heater and toaster; noted as cheapest and most available option
**Mica** — used as the insulating support sheet/ring for nichrome elements in both appliances

items.t: Living In The World
topics: The concept of objectification (from Colin Wilson's work) and how it enables harm
The human/animal distinction as a rationalisation for exploiting nature
How consumerism depends on objectifying the natural world
Robert's broader argument about "purpose of life" (referenced as a companion video)
The environmental consequences of objectification (ocean plastic, strip mining)
An alternative worldview: humans as *part of* nature, not separate from it

items.t: 2138 Choosing Free CAD Software For 2023
topics: Why people's CAD advice reflects their own experience, not yours
Questions to ask yourself before choosing: why do you want CAD, what are your goals?
Skill-level tiers: beginner, intermediate, and advanced
Five key selection criteria: functionality, ease of use, project requirements, community support, and scalability
Specific software recommendations per skill level
Robert's own CAD journey: starting with TinkerCAD, considering a move to OnShape

items.t: Genes, Intelligence, Scientific Philosophy And Being A Cat
topics: The limited role of genes vs. the primacy of environment in development
The thalidomide example as evidence that womb environment overrides genetic instructions
The nature vs. nurture debate and its 200-year history
Intelligence redefined as the ability to map behavior to environmental contours
Learning as the organism adapting to its environment (physical development as a form of learning)
Kinesthetic, auditory, and visual learning styles; the Bobath technique in physiotherapy
The idea that intelligence is distributed across all things (rivers, rocks, cats)
The self vs. serving a larger purpose — social and philosophical implications
The philosophy underpinning scientific investigation and how it directs results
Energy use and microgeneration as a practical example of working with rather than against the environment
Robert's open-source, collaborative ethos as a direct expression of his philosophy
materials: **Thalidomide** — drug given to pregnant women (primarily in the UK) that altered the womb chemical environment, causing babies to be born without limbs despite unchanged genes

items.t: 2136 The Fundamentals Of 3D Printers
topics: The four main types of 3D printing, with focus on Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) / Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)
How filament printers use X, Y, and Z axes to position the extruder in 3D space
The hot end, feed mechanism, and nozzle — and how nozzle size determines print resolution
Filament materials, with PLA highlighted as the most popular
Additive vs. subtractive manufacturing (3D printing vs. CNC routing)
How to obtain a 3D model: designing from scratch or downloading from model repositories
Slicing software: how it converts a 3D model into G-code layer instructions
Infill patterns and percentages
Support structures for overhangs and protrusions
Print speeds and cost tiers for consumer machines
3D pens (Scribe 3D, 3D pen, 3Doodler) as a simplified analogue
materials: **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — described as the most popular filament; relatively easy to print with, though noted to have some drawbacks
**Thermoplastic (generic)** — the class of material used in FDM printing; heated and extruded through the nozzle

items.t: Fear And Formaldehyde
topics: The nature of chemicals: everything is a chemical, so the real question is where to draw the line between harmful and harmless
Misinformation and knee-jerk reactions to chemical hazards (using lead paint remediation as an example)
How to inform yourself: reading warning labels and manufacturer safety data sheets (MSDS)
Contextualising formaldehyde's cancer risk: only linked to rare nasal cancer after prolonged, heavy occupational exposure
Formaldehyde's natural occurrence and widespread everyday uses
The importance of proportionate, "middle road" thinking when assessing risk
materials: **Formaldehyde (formalin)** — the central subject; a 37% aqueous solution (formalin), technically present as methylene glycol in water; naturally produced by the body and found in apples and pears; used in wood glues, dinner plates, Formica, dishwashing sponges, and formerly as a hospital disinfectant
**Methylene glycol** — the form formaldehyde takes when dissolved in water
**Lead / lead paint** — used as an example of mismanaged chemical risk; lead remediation causing more poisoning than leaving paint undisturbed
**Sulfuric acid (98%)** — cited as an example of a chemical whose danger is unambiguous and universally acknowledged
**Water** — used to illustrate that all substances are chemicals
**Potassium** — mentioned in the context of banana overconsumption as a humorous dose-makes-the-poison example

items.t: 2134 DIY Induction Heater - The How, The Why and The Wherefore
topics: Eddy currents: what they are and how they form in metals
How moving magnetic fields (permanent or electromagnetic) induce electron flow and heat
The four core components of an induction heater: power supply, coil, workpiece, and electronic switch
Tank (resonance) circuits and why they improve efficiency
Zero-voltage switching and why it reduces power loss in MOSFETs
Circuit design based on a flyback oscillator topology
Component selection and ratings
Practical demonstration of heating metal and a teaspoon
materials: **Metal workpiece** — the object being heated by induced eddy currents
**Ferrite core** — mentioned as something removed from the flyback circuit to adapt it for induction heating

items.t: Creativity
topics: The "sledgehammer" approach to creativity (Edison, Linus Pauling) and its limitations
Creativity as a muscle that must be exercised, not a fixed trait
How Western education suppresses creative thinking through rote repetition
The importance of noticing and observing the world as the foundation of invention
How social environment and others' opinions shape one's creative self-image (Colin Wilson)
Reframing "good" vs "bad" ideas as "appropriate" vs "inappropriate"
Community and mutual respect as enablers of creativity

items.t: 2132 A Split Ring Epicyclic (Harmonic) Gear
topics: History of harmonic/strain wave drives (developed by C.W. Musser in the 1950s)
Operating principles of harmonic drives: wave generator, flex spline, and circular spline
Advantages of harmonic drives: high torque density, zero backlash, compactness, precision
Disadvantages: manufacturing complexity, limited angular range, lower efficiency, flex spline fatigue/cracking
The split ring epicyclic gear as a solution to those disadvantages
Gear tooth count design: 18-tooth sun, 21-tooth planets, 57-tooth and 60-tooth ring gears (3-tooth difference)
Use of slightly different gear moduli (2.0 vs 1.98) to allow the two ring gears to mesh with the same planets
Massive gear reduction achievable with this design
3D printability and practical demonstration
Use of animation in the video (Robert soliciting viewer feedback on it)
materials: Skateboard bearings (22 mm × 8 mm × 7 mm thick) used for the central drive shaft

items.t: 039 Charge Controllers
topics: How charge controllers work at their most basic level
Electromechanical voltage regulators: solenoids, springs, and self-oscillation
The role of capacitors in suppressing "hunting" (oscillation instability)
Car alternators as practical examples of built-in charge control
Dump loads as a method for dissipating excess energy
Philosophical digression: "sensing" vs. intrinsic behavior in electrical systems (light switch and Jacquard loom analogies)
Three-relay electromechanical regulator configurations (cutout, voltage, current)
Modern solid-state approach: separating sensing (resistive voltage divider) from switching (transistor)
Arduino-based voltage regulator for controlling an alternator field winding
MPPT as a popular but not universally necessary feature — understanding your own needs matters
materials: **Solenoid** — used to demonstrate electromechanical voltage regulation; coil of wire with a magnetizable rod
**Rubber band / spring** — provides restoring force in the solenoid demo
**Capacitor** — placed across the coil to prevent "hunting" (rapid uncontrolled oscillation)
**Magnets** — used in the self-oscillating regulator demo
**Lead acid battery** — used as the target charging application; requires ≤14 V
**Car alternator** — shown as a real-world device containing an internal voltage regulator
**Diode and resistor** — used inside the alternator to set the voltage regulation threshold
**Pre-built voltage regulation board (buck/boost type)** — cheap off-the-shelf module for stepping voltage down
**Transistor (large, with heatsink)** — used as the electronic switch for the field winding
**Resistors (voltage divider)** — two resistors in series to scale high voltage down to Arduino logic levels (0–5 V)
**Arduino (Uno)** — microcontroller used for sensing voltage and switching the field winding; uses pin A0 for sensing, pin 13 for switching

items.t: Being Too Rigid
topics: The problem with asking "what is the best?" without defining context
Lithium's limitations as a universal battery material due to scarcity
The difference between ideal and real electronic components
How real resistors, capacitors, and inductors all contain elements of all three passive component types
The structural similarity of batteries, supercapacitors, and solar cells

materials: **Lithium** — high energy density battery material, but too scarce for global EV adoption
**Carbon, iron, aluminium, magnesium** — more abundant alternative battery electrode materials
**Electrolyte** — present in batteries; can also be present in capacitors
**Insulating membrane/separator** — microporous or nanoporous, used in both batteries and capacitors
**Metal plates** — standard electrode material in capacitors, batteries, and solar cells
**Tap water** — used by a viewer as an improvised electrolyte in a stripped-down version of Robert's design

items.t: 2131 Thermoelectric Generators And Graphite Paint

Thermoelectric generators (TEGs) and how they work

materials: **Rustin's G-Shield graphite paint** — conductive coating, 10 Ω/sq at 100 µm, used as the graphite electrode
**Graphite** — described as naturally exfoliated in G-Shield; the primary conductive material
**Aluminium foil** — used as the second conductor in the thermocouple junction
**Bismuth telluride** — mentioned as a common high-efficiency TEG material (not used here)
**Lead telluride** — mentioned as another conventional TEG semiconductor (not used here)
**Zinc** — noted as a candidate alternative to aluminium for the foil electrode
**Nickel** — noted as another candidate foil material for future experiments
**Bronze, copper** — mentioned as possible graphite pairing materials

**Copper tape (slug tape)** — adhesive-backed copper used as electrode strips

items.t: 2123 The Holcomb Energy System
topics: Introduction to Holcomb Energy Systems and its founder Dr. Holcomb

Dr. Holcomb's transformer demonstration (plexiglass vs. electrical steel)

items.t: 2120 Time For Change

items.t: 2119 DIY Magnetic Switch

items.t: 2118 Permanent Magnets You Can Turn Off And On

items.t: 038 All About Starlite And Invention

materials: **Sugar** — used as the primary intumescent/carbonizing agent in the DIY coating

items.t: 'No New Knowledge Here' - A Little Bit On How To Make A Start

items.t: Don't Do That It Will Kill You - The Dumbest Thing You Can Say

items.t: You Don't Need To Know In Order To Do

items.t: Sitting On Your Keister

items.t: Science Is Belief

items.t: 2090 Simple Machines And Levers

items.t: The Law Of Unexpected Consequences

items.t: A Small Nuclear Bomb To Save The World
topics: Geoengineering as a climate change intervention strategy

Heat capacity comparison: sand/clay/concrete (~850 J/kg/°C) vs. water (~4,000 J/kg/°C)

materials: **Sand** — primary thermal storage medium; can withstand 500–1200°C
**Resistance wire (Nichrome/kanthal-type)** — heating element wire from toasters, ovens, kilns, vapes

**Copper** — coil used to extract heat from a sand battery via water circulation
**Zeolite / calcium chloride** — mentioned as an alternative thermochemical storage system
**Clay and concrete** — noted as comparable thermal storage materials (~850 J/kg/°C)

**4×4 (100×100 mm), 3 mm thick square steel tube** — rocket stove construction material

items.t: 2076 The Magic Button Generator

Coil (wound on a coil former)

items.t: 029 Printing Generators

**Microwave oven capacitor (1 µF, 2 kV)** — used as the storage capacitor in the circuit
**Microwave oven diodes** — used in the rectification circuit
**Carbon felt** — tested as a triboelectric rubbing material; produced ~6 V pre-charge
**Cotton wool** — tested as a triboelectric rubbing material; produced ~4 V

items.t: 028 Unlocking Energy From The Sky - The Fractal Capacitor

items.t: 2075 The Zephyr Windbeam - A Micro Wind Energy Generator

items.t: 027 The Partisan Battery And Radio Lamp

**Graphene** — noted as a future material under investigation for higher-efficiency thermoelectrics

items.t: 2074 Windbelt DIY

items.t: How To Do Everything

items.t: 2073 The Concrete Supercapacitor From MIT

items.t: Waste - A Resource

**Hot glue** — used to mount coils to the baseboard

items.t: Nuclear Power
