**Plasma** — ionised gas of positively charged particles used as the fusion medium

items.t: 2065 DIY Moldable Rubber For 3D prints (Homemade Sugru Oogoo)
topics: History and background of Sugru, invented by Jen Ní Ghailín and launched in 2010

items.t: 2064 Lost Secrets in the Art of Battery Making
topics: The philosophy of recovering lost technical knowledge from historical publications
The Bennett Battery (zinc-iron in KOH) and its lineage toward nickel-cadmium batteries
The Lalande Battery using a copper oxide depolarizer paste on iron
The Chromate/Dichromate cell: a high-energy-density but hazardous battery type
The Daniel cell and its gravity-battery variant using activated carbon to stabilise boundary layers
The historical progression from the voltaic pile → Daniel cell → gravity battery → nickel-iron → NiCd
materials: **Zinc** — anode material; ideally amalgamated or alloyed to suppress hydrogen evolution
**Mercury** — historical amalgamation agent for zinc; noted as hazardous and no longer used
**Iron / steel wool** — cathode material in the Bennett Battery
**Carbon rod / carbon felt** — electrode material used across multiple battery types
**Potassium hydroxide (KOH)** — electrolyte (225 g in 500 mL water) for Bennett and Lalande cells
**Black copper oxide (CuO)** — depolarizer in the Lalande Battery paste; sourced as pottery glaze
**Magnesium oxide (MgO)** — component of Lalande paste; acts as a geopolymer binder
**Magnesium chloride (MgCl₂)** — component of Lalande paste; geopolymer activator
**Potassium dichromate** — electrolyte base for the chromate cell; REACH-regulated since 2017, carcinogenic
**Sodium dichromate / chromic acid** — alternative chromate cell electrolytes mentioned
**Sulfuric acid (98%)** — used in chromate electrolyte (8.5 mL) and historically for zinc amalgamation
**Potassium nitrate** — 16 g added to chromate electrolyte "secret sauce" to increase activity
**Copper sulfate** — cathodic electrolyte in Daniel cell variant
**Zinc sulfate (1 molar)** — anodic electrolyte in Daniel cell variant
**Activated granulated carbon** — used in Daniel cell to adsorb and stabilise the copper sulfate boundary layer; sourced as pond filter media
**Nickel** — briefly trialled as electrode, observed to form nickel hydroxide surface layer
**Cadmium** — mentioned as the substitution that produces a NiCd battery

items.t: Nine Of The Strangest Renewable Energy Sources You Have Probably Never Heard Of

**Green fluorescent protein (GFP)** — extracted from jellyfish, applied to solar cells as a light-harvesting sensitiser
**Chlorophyll** — mentioned briefly as a prior analogous biologically-sourced solar material

items.t: 016 The Dumas Effect - A Free Energy Water Heater
topics: History of the Dumas Effect and its independent rediscoveries (Dumas, Peter Davey, George Wiseman)
Open-source vs. patent-locked approaches to invention

**Gold-cadmium** — early shape memory alloy (noted from 1932 research)

**Cobalt** — catalyst for converting syngas into liquid hydrocarbons (ethanol, methanol, kerosene, petrol)

items.t: The Stirling Engine A Real Alternative

items.t: What Is Counter Torque And Where Does It Come From

items.t: 009 The Folding Wind Turbine

items.t: 007 Graphene - Making it And Using it

items.t: 1803 The Rodin Coil And How To Wind It

items.t: 1802 The Faulhaber Coil - An Error Correction And An Apology

items.t: 1801 Winding Star (Faulhaber) Coils And Making Litz Wire

items.t: 1745 Rocket Stoves And The Law

items.t: 1742 The Rocket Stove - Explained and Demystified #rocketstove
topics: The Argand lamp as a historical analogy: a glass tube over a flame dramatically improving airflow and combustion
Why open wood fires are only ~15% efficient: pyrolysis gases (volatile organics) escape before burning
The three stages of wood combustion: dehydration (~100°C), pyrolysis gas release (~200–300°C), and combustion (~600°C)
How containing gases in a tube or box raises efficiency to 70–80%+
History of the rocket stove: Larry Winiarski / Aprovecho, ~1980; the Vita stove; Roman hypocaust inspiration
The J-tube design and the role of insulation
Mass heaters vs. direct stoves: a mass heater shifts heat delivery in time, it does not create more energy
Energy vs. power distinction: burning fuel fast gives the same total heat, just delivered quicker
Temperature trade-offs: very high temperatures (up to 1200°C) require exotic materials and shorten stove life
Why the stove is called "rocket" (likely the distinctive roar, or just marketing)
materials: **Wood** — primary fuel; contains water, volatile organics released during pyrolysis
**Volatile organic compounds from wood** — alcohol, sugar, methanol; released at 200–300°C and combustible if captured
**Wax** — historical pre-1780 lighting medium used as a reference point
**Steel** — used in the original Vita stove (a "collar of steel around the fire")
**Bricks** — thermal mass in Economy 7 heaters and rocket mass heaters

items.t: 1741 How To Change A Rocket Stove For Indoor Use #rocketstove
topics: Why a bare rocket stove cannot be used directly in a house chimney (all heat escapes straight up)
The baffle box principle: enclosing the stove chimney forces gases to recirculate and surrender heat before exhausting
Cutting down the stove chimney height from 800mm to 500mm to fit the intended application
Fabricating a steel box enclosure from sheet steel (200×500mm plates)
Tack welding vs full welding as a strategy for alignment and correction
Attaching a flue pipe outlet to connect to a house chimney

Optional heat exchanger/manifold arrangements for extracting more heat from exhaust gases
Testing draw direction with the box fitted
materials: Sheet steel plate — 200×500mm sections used to fabricate the baffle box
Steel pipe — welded on as the flue outlet connector
Insulation — mentioned as an optional addition inside the box (Robert is skeptical of significant benefit)

items.t: 1690 Kilowatt Labs 3.5kWh Capacitor Module - Teardown
topics: Teardown of a Kilowatt Labs Series 3550 capacitor module (48V, 3.5kWh rated)
Cell configuration analysis: 1200 cells in a 20-series × 30-parallel × 2-layer arrangement
Voltage anomaly: 2.4V per cell, below the 2.7V minimum for any supercapacitor
Weight anomaly: ~43g per cell vs ~30g expected for supercapacitors; matches 18650 lithium batteries (~45g)
Size anomaly: cells measure 18mm × 65mm — exactly the 18650 battery form factor
Energy density fraud: claimed ~63 Wh/kg vs actual supercapacitor maximum of ~10–12 Wh/kg (Skeleton Technologies top-end)
Maximum discharge rate mismatch: rated 1.7C is consistent with LTO batteries, not supercapacitors (capable of 100–200C)
Price comparison: supercapacitor storage costs ~£60,000–70,000/kWh vs LTO at ~£150–200/kWh
Cycle life difference: supercapacitors ~1,000,000 cycles vs LTO ~7,000–10,000 cycles
Risk of deep discharge damage to lithium cells (unlike true supercapacitors)
Intended application: solar energy storage
White-labelling: the module appears to be a Chinese-manufactured product rebranded by Kilowatt Labs (a US company)
materials: **Lithium titanate (LTO)** — the battery chemistry Robert suspects is inside, matching on voltage (2.4V), weight, size, and energy density
**18650 cells** — the cylindrical battery form factor (18mm diameter × 65mm height) that the internal cells match exactly

**Lithium hexafluorophosphate in acetonitrile** — the standard electrolyte used in conventional supercapacitors (mentioned in comments; Robert notes this makes claims of "lithium doping" as a differentiator meaningless)
**Lithium oxide-based cathode** — component of LTO batteries (mentioned in comments)
**Graphite, aluminium, copper** — other internal materials of LTO cells (mentioned in comments)
**Maxwell supercapacitors** — referenced for comparison (5,000–10,000 Farad units, ~600 GBP each)
**Skeleton Technologies supercapacitors** — referenced as the real-world top-end benchmark (~7–12 Wh/kg)

items.t: 1667 Aluminium Gallium And Hydrogen

items.t: 1657 Printing Copper - Amazing Things To Do With A Gweike Cloud Pro 1
topics: Laser reduction of copper(I) oxide to metallic copper
Making a DIY conductive ink from pottery glaze, water, and matte medium
Rod coating technique for applying uniform ink layers
Optimizing Gweike Cloud Pro laser settings (speed, focal offset, power)
Applications: printed circuit boards, motor stators/rotors, axial flux generators
Comparison to traditional PCB etching with ferric chloride
materials: **Copper(I) oxide (red copper oxide)** — pottery glaze used as the copper source; reduces to metallic copper under laser heat
**Matte medium** — acrylic binder (~20% by weight) used to suspend the oxide powder into a paintable ink
**Water** — diluent for the ink
**Plastic sheet (from salvaged LCD TV)** — substrate for the coating
**Ferric chloride** — mentioned as the conventional (wasteful) PCB etchant this method could replace

items.t: 1652 Glowforge Pro Versus Gweike Cloud Pro
topics: The rapid growth of the desktop laser cutter market (~$3.3B in 2020, projected ~$6.6B by 2022)
Overview of the Gweike Cloud Pro's physical construction (all-metal, stepper motors, linear rails)
Comparison of work area sizes (Gweike slightly larger than Glowforge)
Included rotary tool attachment and honeycomb bed
Internal cooling system vs. older water-pump designs
Camera systems: Gweike's single-camera simulated autofocus vs. Glowforge's dual-camera true autofocus
Software flexibility: Gweike supports cloud, standalone, and LightBurn; Glowforge is cloud-only
Support quality and community size for each machine
Price comparison: Gweike Cloud Pro ~$3,200 USD vs. Glowforge Pro ~£6,000
Glowforge's paid subscription model for full design library access
materials: Wood (referenced for maximum cutting depth: ~12–13mm)

items.t: 1653 Solar Panel Myths That Just Won't Die 1 - Recycling
topics: The myth that solar panels cannot be recycled
Current solar panel replacement rates in Europe (1% now, projected ~40–46% by 2040)
European recycling mandates vs. lower rates in Australia (17%) and the US (10%)
The true lifespan of solar panels (~40 years) vs. the commonly cited 25–30 year figure
The second-hand solar panel market
Material composition of a solar panel and recyclability of each component
Economic value of the solar panel recycling industry ($170M now, projected $2.7B by 2040)
A French company developing a lower-cost recycling process
The possible origin of the myth from wind turbine blade recycling issues
materials: **Glass** (~76% of panel weight) — crushed into cullet; ~96% recycled
**Aluminium** (~10%) — frame material; 100% recycled
**Silicon** (~5%) — photovoltaic cells; ~80% recaptured and reused
**Plastic** (~10%) — encapsulant/backsheet
**Silver** (~1% of "other metals") — used for conductive traces on silicon cells; 130–400 mg per panel, a key economic driver for recycling
**Lead** — mentioned in comments as present in solder in silicon PV cells, though less than in TV sets
**Cadmium** — mentioned in comments as present in cadmium telluride (CdTe) cells but *not* in standard silicon cells

items.t: 1651 Can Solar Power Run The World?
topics: Global energy consumption projections to 2030 (US Department of Energy figures: ~700 quadrillion BTUs / ~200,000 TWh)
Solar irradiance and panel efficiency calculations (~1 kW/m², ~20% efficiency = ~200 W/m²)
Total land area required to power the world with solar (~500,000 km² — roughly the size of Spain)
The "supersite" concept: 5,000 sites of ~10 km per side distributed globally
Sahara Desert as a hypothetical solar resource (9 million km², capable of ~630 TW vs. 15 TW world demand)
Solar panels over US highway infrastructure (~94,000 km²) as a way to generate ~20% of world energy
Golf courses as a reference scale (~40,000 globally = ~10% of needed area)
Wind power as a complementary source (11 million 5 MW turbines to cover world need)
Existing hydro power (~6% of world energy, with caveats about environmental damage)
Nuclear power's limitations: ~2.5% of world energy, only ~80 years of uranium reserves remaining, waste volumes
Energy storage alternatives beyond lithium batteries
Home-scale solar installation viability (~13.7 m² per household at 15 kWh/day)
Solar panel recycling and energy payback period
materials: **Silicon** — ~5% of a solar panel by weight; core photovoltaic material
**Glass** — ~76% of a solar panel by weight; 95% recyclable
**Aluminium** — ~8% of a solar panel by weight; 100% recyclable
**Plastic** — ~10% of a solar panel by weight
**Uranium** — nuclear fuel; estimated ~80 years of reserves remaining at current consumption
**Spent uranium fuel** — nuclear waste product; projected ~240,000 m³ cumulative if nuclear doubles by 2030
**Sand** — mentioned as a thermal energy storage medium (Finnish sand battery)
**Compressed air** — proposed by Robert as a preferred energy storage medium
**Lithium** — referenced critically as an over-relied-upon storage medium; Robert argues it is not the only option
**CO₂** — mentioned as a basis for a battery/storage technology
**Silver / copper** — in solar panel contacts; noted that silver thrifting and copper replacement are expected to reduce material costs

items.t: 1648 DIY Solar Panel From An Old Light Fitting Or LED TV
topics: LEDs as photovoltaic devices (bidirectional semiconductor behaviour)
Light guides in LED lighting panels and LED TVs — same underlying technology
University of Rochester research: 50% output improvement via light channelling rather than focusing
Disassembly of a Gariston 30W flat LED panel to expose its light guide, diffuser, and LED strips
Measured output: ~3.6–3.7V, ~49–50mA
Potential upgrade path: replacing LED strips with gallium arsenide solar cells for much higher efficiency
Cost advantage: same electricity generation achievable with less panel material
materials: **Gariston 30W LED lighting panel** — the main subject, used as a solar generator
**Acrylic light guide** — channels light from edge LEDs across the panel
**White reflective plastic sheet** — reflects light back toward the output face
**Frosted diffuser panel** — removed in the experiment to allow direct light entry
**White LED strips** — edge-mounted; act as the photovoltaic elements
**Gallium arsenide (GaAs) solar cells** — mentioned as a high-efficiency (~40–45%) replacement for the LED strips

items.t: 1647 A Way To Improve Solar PV by 50%
topics: Limitations of current solar PV efficiency and cost
Problems with focused PV (FPV): heat buildup and expensive sun-tracking systems
Light guides as an alternative concentration method (concentrated PV / CPV)
University of Rochester research reporting ~50% improvement per area covered
Practical source of light guides: salvaged LCD screens
Circular light guide designs (e.g., Morgan Solar) concentrating light to a central cell
DIY potential using recycled LCD panels
materials: Acrylic (light guide panel extracted from LCD screens)
Silicon (solar cell material, mentioned in reply)
Gallium arsenide (mentioned in reply as a broad-spectrum, high-cost cell material)
White reflective coating (on the non-exit faces of the light guide)

items.t: 1646 A Truly Amazing Way To Try To Get Free Electricity
topics: Graphene as a marketing buzzword being exploited by product manufacturers
"Colleen" — an Indian sanitary pad brand claiming graphene health and absorption benefits
A viral video falsely demonstrating a graphene-infused pad strip powering a light bulb
The use of a trick "magician's light bulb" to fake the electricity generation demo
Historical parallels to radium-in-toothpaste pseudoscience
materials: **Graphene** — claimed as a functional additive in the sanitary pad; Robert argues it is merely drawn on with a pencil and used as a buzzword
**Radium** — cited as a historical analogy for dangerous/fraudulent ingredient marketing (radium in toothpaste)

items.t: 1643 How To Make A Parabolic Mirror
topics: Motivation: building a solar reflector for potential use with a Stirling engine or steam boiler
Parabola geometry and the mathematical relationship between radius, depth, and focal length (`r² = 4 × focal point × depth`)
How to find/draw a parabola using a flashlight (conic sections demonstrated visually)
Overview of multiple parabolic mirror construction methods
Detailed build using a bicycle wheel, Mylar space blanket, rubber sheet, and tire valve inflation
Brief mention of future plans involving copper nanoparticle flash steam boilers
materials: **Bicycle wheel (spokeless)** — used as the rigid outer rim/frame
**Space blanket (Mylar / aluminised Mylar)** — the reflective mirror surface
**Super glue** — bonding the Mylar to the bicycle wheel rim
**Gorilla Tape** — securing and tensioning the Mylar to the work surface
**Rubber sheet** — used as the inflatable membrane to form the parabolic shape
**Tire valve** — glued to the rubber sheet to allow pump inflation
**Fiberglass** — applied to fix/hold the inflated parabolic form
**Copper nanoparticles** (mentioned in passing) — MIT research context for flash steam boilers; not used in this build

items.t: 1640 DIY Graphene Parabolic Mirror
topics: Mechanical exfoliation of graphite to deposit graphene onto curved surfaces
Graphene's infrared reflectivity (~99.9%) and its potential for solar applications
Using a polypropylene breakfast bowl as a concave substrate
Proof-of-concept solar focusing test
Future directions: applying graphene flat to a sheet, then stretching it into a parabolic shape
materials: **Graphite (5 micron)** — the source material rubbed onto the bowl surface to deposit graphene
**Polypropylene bowl** — chosen as the substrate due to ease of coating compared to acrylic
**Wool felt** — used as the rubbing medium to apply graphite via mechanical exfoliation
**Mylar sheet** — referenced as an alternative reflector material used by another creator (Nighthawk in Lights)

items.t: C0403 Generator And Pump Bicycle
topics: Converting a bicycle into a generator and water pump using a bike exercise stand
Repurposing a salvaged (dead) pump by attaching a metal cog via spot welding
Using a three-phase motor as a generator driven by the bike's rear wheel flywheel
Rectifying and regulating AC output to 5V DC for USB device charging
Dual-mode operation: electricity generation only, or combined pumping and charging
materials: Salvaged water pump (dead/stripped of electrics)
Metal cog (used as drive coupling on the pump shaft)
Three-phase motor (from a previous project, thick windings for high amps/low volts)
Three-phase bridge rectifier
Buck converter (~£1–2)
Female USB connector
Power bank (used as test load)
Exercise bike stand (with heavy flywheel)
Pipe fittings / push-fit pipework and union connector
Wood (used as mounting board)

items.t: C0405 Hammer Mill Bicycle
topics: Biochar production using a converted 250-gallon propane tank
Processing dried biochar through a hammer mill
Bicycle-powered drive mechanism for the hammer mill
Using charcoal-leaf mix as chicken coop bedding to "activate" the biochar
Applying activated biochar to compost/garden as a soil amendment
materials: Wood and organic material (feedstock for biochar)
Charcoal / biochar (end product of the burn)
Water (used to quench coals)
Leaves (mixed with charcoal for bedding)
Chicken manure (used to activate the biochar)

items.t: C0401 Bandsaw Bicycle
topics: Introduction and demonstration of the finished bandsaw
Walkthrough of the adjustable table and blade guides
Drive system: washing machine motor, chain, gears, and derailleur tensioner
Electrical system components and safety measures
Custom clutch mechanism to protect the motor
Build process overview from donor bicycle to finished machine
materials: 16-inch wheel bicycle (donor frame, $12)
Washing machine motor (connected in series)
Microwave oven transformer
Relay
12V DC converter
Capacitors
Full bridge rectifiers
Aluminum strips (to cover wheel grooves)
Springs (motor mounting and chain tensioner)
Bearings: V-slot bearing and linear bearings (for blade guides)
Chain and gear/sprocket set
Bicycle derailleur (repurposed as chain tensioner)
3D printed parts (blade guide bars)

items.t: C0404 Generator Bicycle
topics: Converting a stationary exercise bike into an electricity generator
Comparison of three generator types by output wattage
Charging batteries (lead-acid, cordless tool batteries) via pedal power
Powering small appliances directly from the bicycle generator
Basic circuit components needed to build an energy harvester
materials: Bicycle dynamo (AC, 2.5W output)
Car power window motor (DC, 6W output)
Car windshield wiper motor (DC, up to 48W output)
Lead-acid battery (small, used for charging demo)
Cordless drill/screwdriver battery pack
Electrolytic capacitor (for voltage smoothing)
Diode (to prevent back-feeding from battery)
75W car inverter (plugged into cigarette lighter outlet)
CC (constant current) transformer/step-down converter
Spring (for applying friction tension to wheel)

items.t: C0409 Tumble Dryer Bicycle
topics: Repurposing a bicycle wheel as a centrifugal clothes spin-dryer
Removing bicycle tires using WD-40 and screwdrivers
Using the bare wheel rim as a spinning platform
Attaching a basket to the rim to hold clothing
Inflating an inner tube to help support and stabilise the assembly
materials: WD-40 (used as a lubricant to loosen and remove the tire)
Bicycle wheel / rim
Bicycle tire
Inner tube (used structurally — air inflator passed through a drilled hole to help hold the basket up)
Basket (mounted on the rim; holes drilled top and bottom to allow spinning)

items.t: C0402 Furrow Making Bicycle
topics: Bicycle-based furrow-making tool for gardening
Proof-of-concept demonstration in a garden with limited soil
Adjusting soil moisture to improve furrow formation
Planting seeds in the created furrows
Discussion of potential improvements to the design
materials: Soil (garden soil, used as the medium for furrow-making)
Water (added to improve soil consistency and furrow retention)
Flour (mentioned/used to simulate seeds in the furrow gaps)
Seeds (referenced as the intended planting material)

items.t: C0407 Pink Elephant Bicycle
topics: Coin-operated kiddie ride (pink elephant) as a creative reuse platform
Human-powered electricity generation via a repurposed bicycle drivetrain
Replacing an AC electric motor with a generator
Competition entry for Robert Murray-Smith's challenge
Future project concepts: electric-assist cargo tricycle, pedal-powered blender/ice grinder
materials: AC electric motor (built into the kitty ride, candidate for replacement with a generator)
Child's bicycle (mounted to the ride's crankshaft as the drive mechanism)
LEDs (proposed decoration/load for generated electricity)
LCD screens and audio equipment (used in a prior artistic installation of the same ride)

items.t: C0408 SOS Bicycle
topics: Emergency signalling using a bicycle as an SOS machine
Morse code representation of SOS (three dots, three dashes, three dots)
Reflective tape application to a bike tire
Personal anecdotes about past cycling accidents and injuries (broken ribs, dislocated AC joint)
materials: Reflective sticky tape (described as the most reflective available in the shop, but noted as insufficiently reflective)

items.t: C0406 Lawnmower Bicycle
topics: Repurposing discarded aluminium cans found in the environment
Building a parabolic mirror from a bicycle wheel to heat water
Converting a bicycle wheel into a cooling fan using tape and cloth
Turning a bicycle into a lawnmower as the primary project
First-time metal deformation using heat and brute force
materials: Aluminium cans (collected from littering in the environment)
Tape (used to blade-ify the bicycle wheel into a fan)
Cloth (wound around the wheel for the fan)
Plasticine / modelling clay ("plastiline" in Austrian German)
Metal pipes (flattened and drilled to connect the lawnmower to the bicycle)

items.t: 1635 A Landmark Event And A Thank You  - We Have Reached 250k Subscribers
topics: Reaching 250,000 subscribers milestone
Gratitude to channel members (~1,200 at the time of recording)
Super Thanks as a support/donation mechanism
How viewer questions and comments drive video ideas
The channel operating with just two people (Robert and Luke)
Encouragement to subscribe and keep watching

items.t: 1631 Matt Ferrel Vs Thunderfoot - Is There A Battle Brewing? - Undecided With RMS
topics: Discovery of Thunderf00t's critical videos targeting Matt Ferrell's content
Thunderf00t's argument that Matt Ferrell lacks scientific background and relies on advertising brochures and news headlines for research
Specific factual errors Matt Ferrell made regarding hydrogen in flue gases
Thunderf00t's critique of Matt's "massless battery" (structural battery) video
Matt Ferrell's apology/update video — in which he reportedly corrected himself incorrectly
Commentary on the scale and difficulty of running large YouTube channels
Thunderf00t's open invitation to critique more of Matt's videos
materials: **Hydrogen** — Matt Ferrell incorrectly claimed it could be extracted from incineration plant flue gases; Robert notes hydrogen burns and is not present in flue gases
**Carbon dioxide, nitrogen, water, trace sulfur gases** — the actual composition of flue gases from incineration plants
**Carbon fibre** — mentioned in the context of the structural battery, described as sandwiching conventional batteries between two carbon fibre layers

items.t: 1628 Paper Battery As Powerful As an AA And Activated by Water
topics: The environmental and economic scale of the AA battery market (~$7.48B in 2019, projected to $10.7B)
The recycling problem with disposable alkaline batteries
Robert's enthusiasm for paper as an engineered material and its sustainability credentials
History of paper batteries (1880s dry cells, 1983 carbon-impregnated paper, 1992 electroconductive polymers, 2005 urine-activated battery, 2009 Stanford paper)
Two categories of paper batteries: impregnated vs. coated/printed
Advantages of paper batteries: lightweight, flexible, cheap, scalable, any size, infinite shelf life when dry
Step-by-step replication of the published paper's battery construction
Live demonstration: voltage reading and powering a digital clock with two cells
materials: **Paper (cellulose stock)** — base substrate; hygroscopic, aids ion transport
**Graphite (5–7 micron)** — active material in the cathode ink
**Zinc powder** — active material in the anode ink
**Shellac** — binder in both cathode and anode inks
**Ethanol** — solvent/carrier for the inks
**Polyethylene glycol (PEG)** — plasticizer to keep shellac flexible and prevent full dehydration
**Carbon black (Ketjenblack 600D)** — conductive additive in the current collector ink
**Sodium chloride (table salt)** — 3 molar solution used to pre-soak the paper electrolyte
**Candelilla wax** — component of the waterproof hydrophobic zone coating
**Rapeseed oil** — mixed 50:50 with candelilla wax for the waterproof coating
**Water (tap water)** — activator; wicks into the salt-soaked paper to initiate the electrochemical reaction

items.t: 1625 Starlite, Maurice And The Art Of Inventing
topics: Maurice Ward (inventor of Starlite) as a model for the amateur inventor mindset
The QED documentary "Plastic Fantastic" (1994) as a source of lessons on inventing
The limited correlation between university education and inventorship
An ICI study showing 5 of 6 top patent-holders had no university degree
Professor Tony Chalice (Brunel University) on how formal training can produce "blinkered" thinking
The Edison vs. Tesla debate: trial-and-error vs. theory-driven problem solving
The liquid metal battery inventor who deliberately hired people with no battery experience
Thermoelectric generation breakthroughs achieved by questioning established dogma
The three core traits of inventors: creativity, stubbornness, and doing
The risk of university training suppressing creative, lateral thinking
materials: **Starlite** — referenced as the subject of prior research and the starting point for this video; not discussed technically here

items.t: 1624 Thermoelectric Generators Have Just Made A Huge Leap Forward
topics: History of thermoelectric generation, including the "Partisan Battery" (video 1493), which used zinc-antimony and constantan alloys
Seebeck coefficients — how voltage per degree Celsius is calculated from material pair coefficients
Limitations of silicon-based thermoelectrics (temperature ceiling ~200–300°C)
The conventional assumption that heavy metals like bismuth were optimal — and why it was wrong
New research showing magnesium and magnesium alloys outperform prior materials by 3–4×
Live demonstration: magnesium ribbon + copper point contact, measuring ~150 mV from applied heat
Using a capacitor in parallel to confirm actual energy delivery (measured ~50–64 mV across cap)
Synthesis routes for magnesium antimonate (ball milling, high-temperature sintering)
Potential application on Mars rovers as a heat generator
materials: **Zinc-antimony alloy** — used in the original Partisan Battery (one side of the junction)
**Constantan** (nickel alloy) — the other side of the Partisan Battery junction; suggested as an alternative test material
**Silicon-based thermoelectrics** — current high-performance devices; limited to ~200–300°C
**Bismuth** — previously considered the leading candidate for high-performance thermoelectrics
**Magnesium ribbon** — used in the live demonstration
**Magnesium-aluminium alloy (printer's plate)** — ~95% Mg, 5–7% Al, shown as a sample
**Pure aluminium** and **pure magnesium strip** — shown for comparison
**Magnesium antimony alloy / magnesium antimonate (Mg₃Sb₂)** — the breakthrough material, 3–4× better performance; proposed for Mars rovers
**Copper** — used as the second junction material in the demonstration
**Nichrome** — suggested as an alternative junction material to test
**Kanthal** — suggested as another candidate junction material
**Antimony** — mentioned for doping magnesium
**Antimony trioxide** — mentioned in comments as Robert's available starting material; would require oxidation with nitric acid
**Antimony pentoxide + magnesium oxide** — sintering route to magnesium antimonate (from comments)
**Thallium** — briefly mentioned by a commenter; Robert expressed obvious safety reservations

items.t: 1623 A Guide To Making A Wind Turbine
topics: Why starting with a motor is the wrong approach to wind turbine design
Using local wind speed data as the starting point for design decisions
Horizontal axis wind turbines (HAWT) vs vertical axis wind turbines (VAWT) — trade-offs for built-up areas
Using the Omni Calculator tool to determine turbine dimensions and expected power output
Understanding shaft RPM and torque from the calculator to select an appropriate motor/generator
Gear ratios needed to match turbine RPM to motor rated RPM
Sizing support electronics (inverters, charge controllers) based on expected power output
materials: DC motor/generator (example: ~3,500 RPM, 0.5 Nm torque, 12V, ~130W, ~£40)

items.t: 1622 The Best Insulation For A Rocket Stove And How To Make It
topics: The fire triangle and why insulation is critical to rocket stove efficiency
Carbon foam as the premier insulation material (originally developed by Ford in 1964)
DIY carbon foam synthesis from sugar via a pyrolysis/carbonization process
Reticulated vs. non-reticulated (closed-cell) carbon foam
Using exhaust gas heat for secondary energy extraction (e.g., steam coil) without robbing combustion zone heat
materials: **Sugar** — primary carbon source for foam synthesis
**Water** — solvent for the sugar syrup
**Nitric acid** (8 ml per 500g sugar / 500ml water) — foaming/catalytic agent; ammonium chloride or zinc nitrate cited as alternatives
**Rockwool** — previously used as insulation, noted as non-ideal
**Carbon foam** (phenolic-resin-derived commercially; sugar-derived DIY) — primary insulation material
**Nickel salt** — mentioned as an additive to make carbon foam electrically conductive
**Ammonium chloride / zinc nitrate** — alternative foaming/blowing agents

items.t: 1621 Paper Versus Starlite
topics: History of Starlite: its inventor's secrecy, his unrealistic demands, and how his demonstrations inadvertently proved fire-protection was achievable, inspiring others
Two fire-protection mechanisms: **intumescent** (gas-forming carbon foam pushed outward) vs. **ablative** (layer-by-layer burning, as used in space-race heat shields)
Paper as an ablative fire-protection material — a 5-minute butane torch test burns ~80 sheets while the back remains cool
Enhancing paper with a Starlite-inspired coating (sugar + sodium bicarbonate in water) to add intumescent protection on top of the ablative effect
Commercial Starlite-style intumescent paints now available under many trade names
Potential applications: cheap fire-resistant building panels made from paper coated with sugar/borax or sugar/bicarbonate
materials: **Paper** (described as low-grade wood-pulp paper; Robert notes copy paper contains kaolin) — used as the base ablative material
**Sugar** — carbon-foam-forming agent; painted onto paper as part of the fire-protection coating
**Sodium bicarbonate (bicarbonate of soda)** — CO₂-releasing intumescent additive mixed with sugar
**Borax** — mentioned as an optional additive to further reduce fire spread (would reduce paper needed to ~20 sheets)
**Water** — solvent for the sugar/bicarbonate paint (40 ml to 20 g sugar + 20 g bicarbonate)
**Butane** — fuel for the torch used in the demonstration
**Kaolin** — noted (in comments) as a filler present in copy paper that may improve performance
**Calcium carbonate** — mentioned in comments as a possible additive in latex paint formulations
**Pentaerythritol** — mentioned in comments as a known charring agent; Robert chose sugar instead for accessibility
**Sorel cement** — mentioned in comments as a potentially good option for related applications
**Carbon foams** — referenced from Robert's earlier video series as an effective heat shield

items.t: 1620 Rocket Stove Stirling
topics: Overview and recap of the J-type rocket stove and its vortex airflow mechanism
Overview of the three-piston Stirling engine (Blade Attila / "can-in-a-can" design)
Combining the two devices into a working rocket stove–Stirling engine system
Use of rock wool insulation to maintain temperature differential in cold/breezy conditions
Discussion of the Carnot efficiency limit (~37%) applying to all heat engines
Suggestions for future improvements from community members and Blade Attila himself
Potential for adding a magnet and coil to extract electrical generation
materials: **Rock wool** — used as insulation around the firebox to maintain temperature differential in cold conditions
**Rubber band** — used as the Stirling engine's diaphragm/piston membrane in the current design
**Fire extinguisher body** — used as the outer can of the Stirling engine
**Speaker diaphragm (heat-resistant cloth)** — suggested as a superior replacement for the rubber band

items.t: 1619 Whatever Happened To Starlite The Wonder Material
topics: The history of Starlite and Maurice Ward's background as a hairdresser-turned-inventor
The 1990 *Tomorrow's World* BBC demonstration (egg coated in Starlite, blowtorched, left uncooked)
Testing by the UK Ministry of Defence (Foulness, 1992; Yag laser, 1994) and NASA/White Sands
Ward's extreme secrecy measures and commercially unrealistic demands
The acquisition of the formula by Thermashield (The Machine LLC, California) from Ward's daughters after his 2011 death
The now-crowded market of comparable intumescent coatings
Philosophical lessons: ideas have no value until turned into products; invention is a team effort; over-secrecy kills commercialisation; showing something is possible inspires others to replicate it
materials: **Starlite** — the proprietary intumescent heat-shield coating; reportedly 20–21 ingredients mixed in a specific sequence
**Carbon** — formed when Starlite carbonises under heat; acts as the primary thermal shield
**Cornstarch** — mentioned as a key ingredient in simple replication recipes
**PVA glue** — mentioned alongside cornstarch as sufficient for basic reproductions
**Boron nitride** — mentioned by Robert in comments as having "interesting properties" in this context
**Cornflour and baking soda** — suggested by Robert in comments as a replication starting point

items.t: 1618 Putting A Thermo Electric Generator On Solar Panels
topics: Solar panel efficiency losses due to heat buildup (15–22% efficiency, degrades further when hot)
Thermoelectric generators (TEGs) using the Seebeck effect vs. Peltier coolers — same principle, reverse operation
Live outdoor experiment placing a TEG in direct sunlight with a CPU heat pipe for cooling
Cost analysis: TEGs vs. solar, ~10,000× cost difference per unit energy
Off-the-shelf TEG temperature limits (~275°C, limited by solder)
High-temperature TEG variants for applications like rocket stoves (which reach 400–500°C)
Automotive use of TEGs where cost is less of a constraint
Why the "just stick a Peltier on it" suggestion doesn't work economically
materials: Thermoelectric generator (TEG) / Peltier device — used for Seebeck-effect power generation
P-type and N-type semiconductors — the core elements of TEG/Peltier modules
Black tape — applied to TEG surface to improve heat absorption
Thermal paste — used to improve thermal contact between TEG and heat pipe
CPU laptop heat pipe — used as the cold-side heat spreader
Non-freon refrigerant spray — used to rapidly cool the cold side during the experiment to boost voltage output

items.t: 1617 How To Generate Electricty From A Cup Of Coffee Or A Tea Light
topics: Follow-up to video 1616: converting a Stirling engine from demonstrator to generator
Rim-mounted magnet arrangement for achieving higher rotational speed at the coil
Three factors governing electricity generation: magnetic field strength, speed of changing magnetic field, and number of coil turns
Circuit design: coil → rectifier → capacitor → LED
Voltage output comparison: coffee cup (~0.44V) vs. tea light (~0.7V)
Why Stirling engines are not widely adopted for domestic use (maintenance, "love affair with solid state")
Potential for scaling the design up
materials: Low thermal difference Stirling engine (Amazon product)
Permanent magnets (rim-mounted, north/south alternating)
Solenoid coil (used as the generator coil)
Rectifier (bridge rectifier implied)
Capacitor
LED
Hot coffee / hot water (heat source)
Tea light candle (higher-temperature heat source)

items.t: 1616 Alternative Solar Power - A Stirling Engine And A Cooler
topics: Low-temperature (LTD) Stirling engines and where to buy them
How gamma-type Stirling engines work (hot/cold side air shuttling, piston cycle)
Building a simple evaporative cooler from floral foam and black cloth
Using sunlight + evaporative cooling as a heat source/sink pair to drive the engine
Comparing Stirling engines to Peltier devices and internal combustion engines in terms of efficiency
Potential for attaching a generator to produce electricity
Discussion of inverting the engine and design limitations of the crank
materials: **Floral foam (Oasis)** — used as a water-absorbing reservoir for the evaporative cooler
**Black cloth** — placed on the foam to increase solar absorption and accelerate evaporation
**Water** — soaked into the Oasis foam as the evaporating medium
**Low-temperature Stirling engine (gamma type)** — purchased from Amazon (~£30), the primary experimental device

items.t: 1615 How To Make A Plant Pot Fridge A DIY Desert Cooler
topics: How Peltier-driven mini-fridges work and their real-world temperature limitations
The physics of evaporative cooling and why hotter/drier conditions improve performance
Step-by-step construction of a two-pot desert fridge
Historical and cultural context of pot-in-pot coolers
Optimal operating conditions (temperature, humidity, ventilation)
Cost and performance comparison: DIY pot cooler vs. commercial Peltier fridge
materials: **Terracotta / unglazed clay plant pots** — outer and inner vessel; porosity enables water evaporation
**Oasis (floral foam)** — used as infill between pots to hold a water reservoir (noted as non-ideal, used because it was on hand)
**Coarse sand** — recommended as better infill material for water retention
**Earth / dirt** — traditional infill material
**Cement** — suggested as an inner-pot coating to prevent water penetration and allow use of saltwater

items.t: 1614 How To Get Cold From Hot By Copying Nature

Suitability of various wicking materials (oasis, chromatography paper, household bricks, wool, clay, terracotta)

**Chromatography paper** — cited as superior to oasis for wicking water upward
**Household bricks** — mentioned as another material exhibiting capillary action
**Black cloth** — used as a solar-absorbing surface to drive evaporation
**Felt** — referenced as the wicking liner in conventional swamp coolers
**Wool** — noted in comments as a usable wicking material
**Clay / terracotta pots** — noted in comments as commonly used for evaporative cooling

**Titanium dioxide (TiO₂)** — filler present in copy paper; contributes reflectivity

**Polyethylene sheet** — used as a transparent cover to insulate the paper surface from hot ambient air

items.t: 1610 All About Rocket Stoves

**Lignin** — breaks down in stage 3 (~320°C), producing tars and volatiles
**Cellulose** — carbohydrate component of wood
**Carbon monoxide (CO)** — secondary gas produced during combustion
**Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — combustion product
**Methanol** — volatile secondary gas released during wood pyrolysis
**Methane** — volatile secondary gas
**Ethanol** — volatile secondary gas
**Formic acid** — produced during hemicellulose breakdown
**Acetic acid** — produced during hemicellulose breakdown
**Charcoal** — solid residue after pyrolysis (~20–30% of wood)
**Soot** — unburned carbon particles

items.t: 1537 Turning Cheap Metals Into Expensive Ones - The Catalytic Condenser
topics: Why catalysts are expensive: rhodium, palladium, and platinum dominate industrial catalysis

materials: **Rhodium** — precious metal catalyst; >$500,000/kg; primary target for replacement
**Palladium** — precious metal catalyst; ~$30,000/kg
**Platinum** — precious metal catalyst; more expensive than gold; used in catalytic converters
**Aluminium** — cheap metal (~$200/kg) whose surface is tuned to mimic precious metals

**Silicon** — base layer of the catalytic condenser device
**Hafnium oxide (HfO₂)** — dielectric layer in the thin-film stack

items.t: 1299 Wind Catching - The Future Of Wind Turbines
topics: The historical analogy between oversized 1980s hard drives (prone to catastrophic failure) and increasingly large modern wind turbines
The physics problem with giant turbines: energy scales with the square of blade radius, but weight and cost scale with the cube

The Wind Catcher system: a grid array of many smaller turbines covering the same swept area as a large turbine
Efficiency gains from using the full square area (including corners) vs. a circular swept area
Redundancy benefits: losing one small turbine in an array is trivial vs. losing an entire large turbine
Comparison of design lifetimes: 20–25 years (large turbines, blades replaced ~every 10 years) vs. 50 years (Wind Catcher)
Robert's broader philosophy: cross-pollinating ideas between unrelated fields enriches problem-solving
Brief mention of his own "wind wall" projects as conceptually aligned with Wind Catcher

items.t: 1135 Wind Turbine Mechanical Power Transmission

**"Miracle cleaning solution"** (from video 1037) — applied to remove rust from the metal rim and outer components

items.t: 1093 Wind Turbines - Some Considerations
topics: Energy in wind is set by swept blade area and wind speed — not by machine design
Betz's Law: maximum theoretical energy extraction is 60% of available wind energy
Best commercial turbines achieve ~80% of the Betz limit (~48% overall efficiency)
Worked example: Tesup/Master 940 turbine (0.8 m blade, £380) yields only ~50 W in average UK conditions (4.2 m/s)
Cost-per-watt as the correct metric for evaluating wind generation
Diminishing returns: the final 20% of efficiency gains account for ~80% of engineering cost
DIY builds (repurposed fans, soil pipe, bicycle wheels) as cost-effective alternatives
The HAWT (horizontal axis) vs VAWT (vertical axis) debate dismissed as largely irrelevant
UK planning permission rules: vertical turbines require permission; horizontal ones generally do not
materials: Reclaimed/repurposed fan (used in a DIY turbine build by "Luke")

Nuts, bolts, and welding rods (build materials, ~£5)

M6 nut — used to secure the adaption plate to the motor spindle

items.t: 1090 Graphene And How To Make It - Mechanical Methods
topics: Distinction between top-down (mechanical) and bottom-up graphene production methods
The original Novoselov & Geim sticky tape exfoliation technique
Pencil-on-paper as a simple shear method
Sandpaper/abrasive rubbing method (Japanese research, 4000-grit sandpaper on plastic)
Electrostatic attraction causing graphene to adhere to plastic substrates
Blender-based liquid exfoliation (Trinity College Dublin method)
Three-roll mill method (American contribution) — high shear, low impact
Ball mill method — medium shear, medium impact
Bead stirrer method — low shear, no impact, using glycerin as a carrier
Sonication (bath and horn sonicators) as a shear/impact method
Use of exfoliated graphite as a superior starting material for all mechanical methods
Preview of upcoming electrochemical methods video
materials: **Sri Lankan graphite** — high-quality natural graphite used as the primary starting material
**Sticky tape / sellotape / packing tape** — used in the original Novoselov & Geim exfoliation
**Graphite powder** — used in sandpaper and blender methods
**Sandpaper (4000-grit)** — abrasive used in the Japanese rubbing method
**Plastic substrate** — graphene adheres to it via electrostatic attraction during the sandpaper method
**Wool, cotton, paper** — alternative abrasive/rubbing materials tested
**Alcohol, water, and washing-up liquid** — solvent mixture used in Trinity College Dublin blender method
**Glue / epoxy-type binder** — used in three-roll mill method to hold graphene during shearing
**Glycerin** — used as carrier/binder in bead stirrer method; easier to separate than solid media
**Exfoliated graphite** — pre-expanded graphite (made in video 1083) that makes all mechanical methods more effective
**Pencil (graphite + clay)** — demonstrated as an everyday shear method

items.t: 1089 Hubless Wheel With My New Gear Method

Super glue — used to bond the gear strip to the rim (test piece)
Contact adhesive — mentioned as an alternative bonding agent
Acrylic epoxy — mentioned as an alternative bonding agent
Steel tube / paint metal from a salvaged exercise bike — fabricated into the wishbone
Flat plate from an old winch — used as the rear frame-mounting plate
Nylon-sleeved roller bearings — fitted to guide and constrain the wheel against precession

items.t: 1083 Graphene And How To Make It - Chemical Methods
topics: Top-down vs. bottom-up approaches to graphene production
Hummer's method and the Tour method for graphene oxide synthesis
Intercalation: the mechanism by which acid enters graphite galleries
SAIGO (sulfuric acid intercalated graphite oxide) as a precursor material
Thermal exfoliation of intercalated graphite ("graphite worms")
Historical context: 1800s origins of the chemistry (Hummer/Reverend)
Graphite particle size and its effect on reaction outcome
Safety protocols for working with concentrated acids
materials: **Graphite** — starting material; various particle sizes (5 µm, 45 µm, 0.3 mm) suit different end products
**Concentrated sulfuric acid (96%)** — primary intercalation acid; 100 mL used in demo
**Phosphoric acid** — used in Tour method (10% of acid mixture, replacing some sulfuric acid)
**Potassium permanganate** — oxidizing agent used in Hummer's and Tour methods
**Sodium nitrate** — original oxidizer in Hummer's method; now largely unavailable due to explosive-precursor restrictions
**Hydrogen peroxide** — added at end of Hummer's/Tour method to convert residual manganese dioxide into soluble sulfates for washing
**Sodium carbonate / sodium bicarbonate** — used as spill-kit neutralizer for acid
**Manganese dioxide** — byproduct during reaction (causes green/brown color); removed via H₂O₂ step
**Bromine, chlorine, iodine, ferric chloride** — mentioned as alternative intercalants (NASA research, 1960s)
**Potassium** — intercalated into graphite yields "golden graphite," a room-temperature superconductor (pyrophoric)
**Bisulfate/bisulfide** — the intercalant species when sulfuric acid enters graphite galleries; causes blue color ("blue graphite")

items.t: 986 Making The Joule Thief
topics: Background motivation: the Bedini SSG (Simple Schoolgirl) motor as a related voltage-boost oscillator

History of the Joule Thief: 1930s self-oscillating vacuum-tube boost converter patents, a low-voltage thermoelectric variant (US Patent 4734658), and the ~1999 popularisation via *Practical Everyday Electronics* (Zapanic)
Real-world application: disposable flash cameras use the same self-oscillating voltage boost principle
Operating principle: bifilar coils, a hard-switching transistor (e.g. 2N2222), and the collapsing magnetic field inducing a high-voltage spike
Why ferrite cores outperform steel laminations at the circuit's switching frequency — but steel still works
Bifilar winding technique and the importance of polarity (dot convention)
Forgiving build tolerances: 20–100 turns, any wire gauge, almost any core material
materials: **Ferrite rod** — salvaged from a radio antenna; used as the primary core for the first build
**Laminated steel core** — pulled from a microwave oven transformer; used as the core for the second build to prove flexibility
**Magnet wire / enamelled wire** — approximately 3 m measured and folded in half for bifilar winding
**Plastic tape** — wrapped around the laminated steel core to protect the wire from chafing
**2N2222 transistor** (or similar NPN) — hard-switching element in the circuit
**Resistor** — biasing component visible on the commercial board
**Diode / LED** — D1 in the original circuit; Zapanic's innovation was replacing the diode with an LED to produce light; a 3.2 V LED is used here
**1.5 V battery** — single cell power source
**Bifilar toroid** — used in the commercial reference module shown for comparison

Building a simple wooden jig to ensure a consistent right-angle bend
Heating plastic to malleability with a heat gun
Timing and technique to avoid over-softening the plastic
Comparing the fit and quality of the bent plastic vs. a bent aluminium piece
materials: Plastic sheet (type unspecified) — the primary material being bent
Aluminium sheet — shown as a failed/rejected alternative due to dents and difficulty achieving clean angles
Wood (three pieces) — used to construct a simple bending jig

items.t: Wind Turbine Performance
topics: Reality check on commercial small wind turbine performance vs. rated specifications
Power curves: cut-in speed, rated speed, emergency shut-off speed
Mismatch between grid-scale turbine design assumptions and domestic/rooftop wind conditions
Turbulent, low-altitude wind vs. consistent high-altitude wind
DIY drum generator outperforming commercial units at low wind speeds
Flywheel effect for smoothing turbulent wind output
Low-torque, high-speed design as the preferred approach for small turbines
Vertical axis wind turbines (VAWTs) as better suited to domestic use
Philosophy of open investigation over recipe-following; all designs placed in public domain
Turning negative criticism into productive research
materials: Scrap wood (used to build the prototype DIY generator, cost ~£10)

items.t: Testing The Cable Drum Wind Turbine
topics: Viewer-inspired design: John Wynn's suggestion to mount magnets around the wheel rim
Rim-mounted magnets as a strategy to increase effective RPM without high axle torque
Debunking the "you need high torque" assumption for small wind turbines
Microwave oven transformer repurposed as a generator stator coil
Effect of a warped cable drum causing output flicker (varying magnet-to-coil distance)
Philosophy: if something works when built badly, it will work better when built well
Comparison of horizontal-axis vs. vertical-axis turbine designs
Wind direction as a key constraint for horizontal turbines
Plans to rebuild as a true vertical-axis wind turbine with proper thrust bearings
materials: **Ceramic magnets** — 62 mounted around the rim of the cable drum; ~£10 for 50
**Microwave oven transformer** — repurposed as a pickup coil/stator
**Scrap timber / cable drum** — structural frame, warped from rain exposure
**Super glue** — used in assembly alongside screws
**36-watt LED lighting panel** — used as the load/output test device

items.t: Converting A Light Panel To Run On Batteries
topics: How LED panel lights work internally (AC-to-DC conversion via transformer)
Why battery conversion is straightforward — the panel is already a DC device
Internal construction of a cheap LED panel (reflector, acrylic light guide, diffuser layers)
Series vs. parallel wiring of LED strips and the trade-offs (voltage vs. current)
Practical battery options for powering the rewired panel
materials: LED panel light (budget, ~£12)
Thin pressed steel back panel
Reflective white plastic sheet
Acrylic light guide (pitted, Fresnel lens, or linear pattern)
Frosted plastic diffuser
AC-to-DC transformer (230V in, 27–42V DC out at 900mA)
Red and black extension wire (soldered onto LED strip terminals)
Kapton tape (used to secure wiring; original used Sellotape)
12V car batteries (×2, connected in series for 24V supply)

items.t: Turn An Old Cable Drum Into A Wind Turbine
topics: Converting a found cable drum into a vertical axis wind turbine rotor
Squirrel-cage blade design cut from the drum's own plywood core
Testing rotational inertia and behaviour in low wind speeds
Integration plan: mounting onto an exercise bike flywheel with magnets for generation
Robert's philosophy of experimentation over expertise
materials: Plywood cable drum (salvaged)
Steel rods (part of the cable drum structure)
Plywood strips (5 cm wide, cut from the drum's inner core, used as blades)
Broom handle (used in the test cradle)
Bearings (fitted to the test cradle uprights)
VDU (monitor) stand (repurposed as a support)
Exercise bike frame/flywheel (intended mounting platform)
Magnets (planned for gluing to the flywheel for generation)

items.t: Make A Light Box
topics: Repurposing spare LED ceiling lighting panels as a light box light source
Building a housing box from builders board / Cintra board (uPVC fascia/soffit material)
Cost comparison: DIY (~£14–20) vs. commercial light boxes (£80–100)
Optional use of broken LCD screens as an alternative light source (shown by "Luke")

Cable management and finishing details

materials: **LED lighting panel** — 600×600mm, 36W, 24V, plugs into mains via transformer; the primary light source
**Builders board / Cintra board / uPVC fascia and soffit** — used to construct the box housing; ~50p per metre
**PVC window trim** — used as an internal shelf to seat the lighting panel
**Chrome pipe** — salvaged material used to fabricate handles
**Super glue / crazy glue** — used to join the builders board sections; glue joint reportedly stronger than the plastic itself
**Self-adhesive cable tie mounts** — small sticky squares used for cable management
**Cable ties** — used with the mounts to secure wiring
**Sticky pads** — used to mount the transformer inside the box
**IP-rated cable connector** — fitted through a drilled hole in the rear of the box for the mains cable entry
**Salvaged flex (mains cable)** — used for the power lead

items.t: Archimedes Screw  Water Turbine - Stream Test

Large-scale Archimedes screw installations (e.g., an ~80kW installation on the Thames)

CD discs (mentioned in comments as material used for a related component)
HDPE (suggested in comments as a preferred alternative plastic for vacuum-forming)

items.t: Convert An Exercise machine Into A Wind Turbine - Part 1

Disassembly and inspection of the cross trainer's internals
Overview of previous exercise machine projects (wind generator, hand crank generator, coil/magnet test bed)
Planning a vertical axis wind turbine (VAWT) or horizontal axis wind turbine (HAWT) conversion
Sourcing cheap exercise machines via Gumtree or Craigslist

Magnetic resistance shoe (braking mechanism)
Small electronics/motor controlling resistance distance
Belt/pulley drive system (large driving pulley and idler pulley)
Cutlass pin (fixing point on the frame)

items.t: Exercise Bike Wind Turbine - A Test In The Wind
topics: Follow-up test to the "Make a Wind Turbine from an Exercise Bike" video
Real-world outdoor wind power generation test
AC output directly driving LED strips (no rectifier or smoothing capacitor)
Voltage output: achieving 24V to drive the LED strip

materials: **Exercise bike** — repurposed as the turbine rotor/frame
**Microwave oven transformer (MOT)** — modified to act as the generator coil (primary coil removed, secondary coil used for output)
**2 cm neodymium N35 magnets** — glued around the exercise bike wheel to induce current as they pass the coil
**24V LED strip** — 90 LEDs used as the electrical load for the test

items.t: Charge Controllers - Some Concepts
topics: The philosophical distinction between "sensing" and "intrinsic behaviour" in electrical/mechanical systems
How complexity (many switches in a black box) creates the illusion of mysterious "knowing"
The Jacquard loom as an analogy for programmatic, switch-based logic
A recommended design methodology: define the job first, then find the implementation
Charge controller design as a modular, logical breakdown of tasks
MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) as an example of a feature that may or may not be needed depending on context
The danger of focusing on implementations before understanding requirements

items.t: Charge Controllers - The Early Days
topics: The conceptual simplicity underlying charge controllers
Electromechanical voltage regulators as the historical precursor to modern charge controllers
How a solenoid-spring-switch circuit self-oscillates to regulate voltage
The role of a capacitor in preventing "hunting" (rapid oscillation/arcing)
Dump loads as a way to handle excess energy
Car alternators as a practical example of solid-state charge control
Efficiency limitations of linear/dissipative voltage regulation
Why efficiency matters more for renewables (solar, wind) than for automotive charging
materials: **Solenoid (coil of wire)** — used to demonstrate electromechanical switching
**Steel rod** — magnetised inside the solenoid to create mechanical motion
**Rubber band** — acts as a spring return for the solenoid demo
**Capacitor** — placed across the coil to prevent hunting/arcing
**Magnets** — used in the second demo arrangement
**Light bulb / large resistor** — mentioned as dump load alternatives to dissipate excess energy
**Diode and resistor** — used in the car alternator's internal voltage regulator to set the cutoff voltage
**Lead acid battery** — used as the target battery example (14V charge voltage)
**Car alternator** — demonstrated as a real-world example of a charge controller

items.t: Two Hour Wood Tongue Drum Project
topics: Sourcing free materials from a discarded wooden bed
Repurposing a speaker cabinet as the drum body
Designing and cutting eight keys (one octave) from pine bed slats
Creating bearing blocks with embedded nuts for bolt-on key attachment
Cutting a rebate channel in keys to improve resonance in cheaper timber
Tuning approach: removing/filing material from individual keys
Trade-offs between rebate cuts and slot-based sliding tuning systems
materials: Pine bed slats — primary material for tone keys, salvaged from a discarded double bed
Speaker cabinet/box — repurposed as the resonator body
8 mm bolts (decorative) with repair washers — used to mount keys to bearing blocks
8 mm drill bit — for through-holes in keys and bearing blocks
13 mm drill bit — for counterbore to recess nuts below the surface
Wood glue — for fixing bearing blocks inside the box
Screws — added to reinforce the older/rickety speaker cabinet

items.t: Make A Hank Drum From A Wok
topics: What a hank drum is and how it is traditionally made
Why woks are a practical substitute for propane tanks
Cutting vibrating tongues using a hole saw at an angle
Tuning the tongues by adjusting their length or adding mass
Joining the two wok halves using aluminium welding rod and a hot plate
Final sound demonstration
materials: Aluminium wok (×2) — used as the drum body
Aluminium welding rod (zinc-based solder) — used to fill guide-drill holes and to join the two wok halves
Steel plate — used to press the woks together on a hot plate during joining
JB Weld — mentioned as a possible adhesive alternative for joining the halves

items.t: Very Easy To Make Wood Tongue Drum
topics: Philosophy of making instruments from scavenged or repurposed materials
Why tongue drums are a good craft project (fun, sellable, educational about frequency/vibration)
Construction of a resonance box from salvaged floorboards
Individual key (tongue) design vs. the common single-block approach
Tuning flexibility of the separate-key method
Commercial potential: targeting accessible, affordable instruments over high-end ones
materials: **Oak floorboards** — primary structural material for both box and keys, sourced as salvaged/discounted offcuts
**MDF** — mentioned as an alternative material suitable for tongue drums
**8mm bolts** — used to mount and fasten the keys to the frame
**Tone woods** — referenced as the ideal material for higher-end instruments, contrasted with everyday woods

items.t: All About Making Wood Based Tongue Drums
topics: Relationship between vibrating material length and pitch (shorter = higher note)
Effect of mass on note frequency, illustrated by guitar string thickness
One fixed point (tongue drums, reeds) vs. two fixed points (guitar strings) and how each vibrates
Router cut at the tongue's fixed point to improve resonance and tonal quality
Tuning tongues by removing mass at the tip or base
Role of a resonant/airtight sound chamber in amplifying tone
Design freedom in tongue shape — curves vs. straight lines
Practical limits: minimum note set by physical size of the instrument
materials: **Oak flooring (repurposed floorboards)** — main material used to construct the tongue drum tongues
**Pine bed slats** — used in vice demonstrations to contrast tone with and without a router cut
**Aluminium** — referenced from a prior video where aluminium was used to make a harp on the same principles
**Mahogany** — mentioned in author comments as a preferred wood that Robert had run out of
**PVC** — mentioned in author comments in relation to PVC tongue drums made by viewers

items.t: Making Tongue Drums -  Working Out The Size Of The Tongues
topics: Finding salvaged wooden flooring as a free material source
The relationship between tongue/bar length and resonant frequency
Deriving the material constant `k` from a single sample measurement
Using the formula `L² = k / f` to calculate lengths for any desired note
Building musical instruments: tongue drums, xylophones, glockenspiels, wind chimes, tubular bells
Looking up note frequencies from a reference table (e.g. Wikipedia)
Cutting pieces slightly long and filing down to fine-tune pitch
materials: Salvaged wooden flooring planks (unspecified hardwood)
6 mm aluminium sheet (cut into bars to demonstrate the technique)

items.t: Guitar Picks From Bio Plastics
topics: Bioplastic synthesis from casein
pH chemistry involved in precipitating the plastic
Molding and pressing the bioplastic into a guitar pick shape
Drying and curing the finished picks
Practical demonstration of the picks on guitar
materials: **Tannic acid** (0.3 g) — acts as a cross-linking/tanning agent
**Citric acid** (20 g) — dissolved in 150 mL deionized water; acidic solution that triggers precipitation
**Potassium hydroxide / KOH** (3 g) — dissolved in 400 mL deionized water; creates alkaline base solution (~pH 13–14)
**Casein** (20 g) — milk protein; the primary plastic-forming material
**Deionized water** — used for both solutions; tap water may be a viable substitute

items.t: Making Ink From Acorns - A Bit Of  A Tribute To The Native Peoples Of The USA
topics: Background on iron gall (iron tannate) ink and a previous video on oak apple ink
Native American use of acorns as a food source (leaching tannins to make acorn flour)
Extracting tannin from whole blitzed acorns via simmering
Separating fats/oils from the tannin solution using alcohol (de-emulsification)
Preparing iron(II) chloride from hydrochloric acid and steel wool
Combining tannin extract with iron salt to form blue-black ink
Simple low-tech alternative: long soak with natural layer separation
materials: **Acorns** — primary tannin source, blitzed whole (including shell) then simmered
**Water** — extraction solvent (~500 mL per 50 g acorns)
**Hydrochloric acid (37%, sold as brick cleaner)** — used to dissolve steel wool and produce iron salt
**Steel wool / iron wool** — reacted with HCl to form iron(II) chloride
**Iron(II) chloride** — the iron salt that reacts with tannins to form the ink pigment
**Methanol (or ethanol)** — used to de-emulsify the acorn extract and agglomerate fats
**Acorn fats and oils** — by-product of extraction; noted as edible if acorn is de-shelled

items.t: Mini Wind Turbine From Desktop Fan
topics: Repurposing and creative reuse of cheap consumer electronics
Identifying motor type (DC brush vs. brushless DC) in a disassembled fan
Designing and fabricating large pinwheel-style wind blades from scrap material
Converting a DC motor into a generator with rectified DC output
Wind turbine fundamentals: blade design, mounting, motor conversion, and rectification
Low-cost experimentation as a learning philosophy
materials: USB 4-inch desktop fan (~£3 supermarket purchase) — the base component
Pressed aluminium fan blade — original blade, flattened and reused as a hub template
Thin plastic sheet from LCD/TV screen diffusers — used to make new wind blades
Card — suggested as an equivalent substitute for the plastic sheet
Double-sided sticky tape — used to assemble the pinwheel blades
Bridge rectifier — added to convert AC motor output to DC

items.t: Traditional Iron Gall Ink - Making It And Selling It As An Idea
topics: History and significance of ferrogallic (iron gall) inks — approximately 1400 years old and used in official administrative documents worldwide
The philosophy of understanding material origins: nature → conversion process → useful product
Collecting oak galls (formed when wasps lay larvae in oak trees)
The chemistry of Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ oxidation and why it makes the ink water-insoluble and long-lasting
Three interchangeable tannin sources: oak galls, tea bags, and commercially purchased gallic acid powder
The role of gum arabic as a binder
The official US government specification for iron gall ink
Adding a temporary dye (methylene blue) to improve immediate visibility during writing
Commercial potential of handmade iron gall ink (~$100/250 ml retail value)
materials: **Oak galls** — primary source of gallatanic (tannic/gallic) acid; collected from oak trees in autumn/winter
**Gallatanic acid / tannic acid** — the reactive tannin component of the ink; also available as a commercial brown powder
**Tea bags** — alternative source of tannin
**Ferrous sulfate (FeSO₄)** — iron(II) salt; the iron source; available as garden fertilizer
**Ferrous chloride** — alternative iron(II) salt, made from wire wool dissolved in hydrochloric acid (brick cleaner)
**Ferrous nitrate** — mentioned as another viable iron(II) salt
**Gum arabic** — binder; derived from the acacia (*Vachellia*) tree; available as powder or amber liquid
**Methylene blue** — added dye to improve immediate writing visibility
**Methylene violet** — mentioned as a common alternative to methylene blue
**Aniline blue dye** — specified in the US standard (3.5 g/L); fades over time as the iron darkens
**Hydrochloric acid** — used in the US specification to stabilize the ink and slow oxidation
**Carbolic acid (phenol)** — used in the US specification as a preservative to prevent microbial growth
**Wire wool** — used with HCl to produce ferrous chloride DIY

items.t: Making Printing Ink From Old Toner And An Introduction To Functional Inks
topics: The two main categories of ink: water-based (writing) and oil-based (printing)
Functional inks and their applications in printed electronics
The role of boiled linseed oil as a drying oil vehicle
Using laser toner as a fine carbon pigment source
Ink mixing techniques: palette knife, muller, and three-roll mill
Ink additives: extenders, soap (release agent), rosin (tackifier), and drying agents
Troubleshooting ink formulation (bleed-out, slow drying, adhesion)

materials: **Boiled linseed oil** — drying oil vehicle/binder; oxidises in air to form a hard, waterproof film
**Laser toner** — fine carbon-based pigment; available in black and colours
**Lamp black** — traditional carbon pigment made by soot deposition
**Iron oxide** — additive for magnetic functional inks
**Graphite** — additive for conductive functional inks
**Cadmium sulfate** — additive for solar-active functional inks
**Rosin** — tree sap powder; used as a tackifier and to improve hardness/scratch resistance
**Soap** — release agent to aid ink transfer from printing block to substrate
**Lead salts** — historically used as driers (noted as no longer usable)
**Cobalt/manganese salts** — modern drying agents (Rustins turbine dryer with cobalt mentioned)
**Gum arabic** — noted in a comment as the binder for newsprint ink (black + water + gum arabic)
**SBR/CMC** — recommended in comments as a flexible binder alternative

items.t: Make A Pen From An Old Can - Easy And Fun

Step-by-step construction of a dip pen nib from aluminium sheet

**Masking tape** — used to secure the nib to the stick handle
**Chopstick (or any stick)** — used as the pen handle
**Wet and dry sandpaper (P400 grit)** — used to smooth/sharpen the nib edge
**Ink** — poured into the can-bottom inkwell for dipping

items.t: Found Art Making A Deer's Head

Picasso's *Bull's Head* as the direct inspiration for the piece

Seat bracket

items.t: Make A Wind Turbine From An Old Exercise Bike

Exploratory disassembly and assessment of an unusual 1980s-era exercise bike with an air-resistance flywheel
Modifying the pressed-steel flywheel into fan blades for wind capture

items.t: Playing With Coils And Magnets

Repurposed exercise bike frame

items.t: 700 Exercise Bike Generator As A Test Bed

items.t: 725 A Printed Generator - No Copper, No Coils, No Wire, No Magnets

items.t: Rotating Ventilator For Wind Power Part 5 - Finishing And Testing

items.t: Rotating Ventilator For Wind Power Part 4 - Making The Rotor
topics: Adapting the stator jig to serve as a rotor-building jig
Using a desktop fan as the rotor's central component
Constructing the rotor hoop from Sintra board (PVC) and ABS strip

items.t: Rotating Ventilator For Wind Power Part 3 - Making The Stator
topics: Disassembly and analysis of the ventilator's internal drum structure

items.t: Contra Rotation With A Pulley And Belt
topics: Contra-rotation pulley mechanism as a foundational mechanical concept

items.t: Making Santizer From Salt, Water And Things Around The Home
topics: Critique of a £20 commercial "make your own sanitizer" kit being sold during the COVID-19 second wave
Electrolysis of salt water to produce sodium hypochlorite

Electrode material selection for home electrolysis
DIY electrolyzer construction from cheap materials
materials: **Tap water** — 250 ml, the solvent base
**Table salt (NaCl)** — 10 grams, dissolved in the water to make brine
**Sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl)** — the sanitizer product formed; equivalent to ~0.8% household bleach
**Graphite/clay pencil lead** — used as electrodes; specifically HB pencils or carpenter's pencils noted as effective

items.t: From Cat Litter To Art Piece - Pottery Without A Kiln - 2 - the recipe
topics: How to identify the correct type of cat litter to use (clumping/clay-based only)
The full recipe with proportions for each ingredient
Mixing and kneading technique to produce a workable dough-like material
Sculpting a face mask as a demonstration piece
Oven curing vs kiln firing
The material's nature as a geopolymer rather than vitrified clay

materials: **Cat litter (clumping, clay-based)** — 600 g; used as the clay source; must be clumping type (not chalk/quartz or wood-based varieties)
**Bentonite** — mentioned as one type of clay found in some cat litters; produces a lighter grey colour
**Sodium silicate / sodium metasilicate** — 120 g powder, made up to a 40% by-weight solution in 500 mL water; acts as the geopolymer binder; polymerises to silica on curing
**Magnesium oxide** — stated as 500 g during mixing (03:41) and restated as 600 g in the closing recap (07:40); used as a filler/thickener, analogous to flour in bread dough
**Chalk and quartz sand** — found in non-clumping lightweight cat litters; explicitly to be avoided
**MDF board** — used as a slightly absorbent, non-stick working surface
**Water** — 500 mL, used to make up the sodium silicate solution

items.t: All About Magnesium Oxide Cements
topics: Fundamental difference between cements (crystal growth and interlocking) and geopolymers (polymerization reaction)
CO₂ problem with Portland cement manufacturing and motivation for alternatives
History of Sorel cements (~150 years old, attributed to chemist Sorel)
Zinc oxide + zinc chloride as the original Sorel cement
Magnesium oxide + magnesium chloride cement (historical use: billiard balls)
Magnesium oxide + magnesium sulfate (Epsom salts) cement
Water sensitivity as the shared weakness of chloride- and sulfate-based Sorel cements
Phosphate-based MgO cements as a water-resistant alternative, and the phosphate scarcity concern
Boric acid as a retarder for phosphate cements
Newer MgO cement variants: MgO + magnesium carbonate, activated MgO (surface-hydroxylated), and MgO + fumed silica
Setting time control through water content
materials: **Zinc oxide (ZnO)** — powder; original Sorel cement base; also used in suntan cream and paints; cheap and widely available
**Zinc chloride (ZnCl₂)** — used as saturated solution to activate zinc oxide cement; causes very fast set at high concentrations
**Magnesium oxide (MgO)** — primary cement base for most recipes discussed; white powder
**Magnesium chloride (MgCl₂)** — activator for MgO cement; same leaching problem as zinc chloride version
**Magnesium sulfate (Epsom salts, MgSO₄)** — used at 40% by weight with 60% MgO; water-activated dry blend
**Ammonium phosphate** — insoluble phosphate hardener for water-resistant MgO cement
**Potassium phosphate** — alternative insoluble phosphate hardener; the widely known internet recipe
**Boric acid** — added to phosphate/MgO mixes as a set retarder to improve workability
**Magnesium carbonate** — used in a newer MgO cement formulation with water
**Fumed silica (SiO₂)** — silicon dioxide used at 10–40% with MgO; large surface area improves bonding; mixed with water into a gel before combining with MgO
**Calcium oxide (CaO)** — basis of Portland cement; mentioned for contrast, not used in demonstrations
**Water** — activator and set-time controller in most recipes

items.t: Pottery Without A Kiln - 1 - The Material

**Bentonite and zeolite** — noted as naturally occurring aluminium silicates

items.t: How To Make A Seaweed Bioplastic -  The Basics

items.t: Improved Solar Still For Fresh Water

items.t: The Perpetual Battery And Wave Generator - How I Think They Might Work
topics: Charge separation produced when a salt-water drop rolls across a graphene surface (referencing ~2015 papers)
Triboelectricity as the conceptual parallel (e.g. rubbing plastic with silk; Kelvin's water dropper)
Clark Patent No. 4,153,757: resistance imbalance as the key to harvesting charge separation
Resistance as a function of both material resistivity and electrode geometry
Asymmetric electrode design (thinner upstream, thicker downstream) as a path to better performance
Surface area as a critical factor for generator output
Graphite-loaded clay plates as an improved electrode material
Early-stage nature of the technology and encouragement to experiment
materials: **Salt water / ionic fluid** — the moving medium that generates charge separation
**Graphene** — surface on which charge separation from rolling water droplets was originally studied
**Graphite-loaded clay rods** — current electrodes used in both devices (graphite + clay + wax binder; acknowledged as suboptimal)

**Wax** — binder in current rods, identified as limiting performance
**Plastic and silk** — cited as a simple triboelectric analogy
**Mercury** — mentioned historically (first electric motor used wire + mercury pool)
**Nitinol** — mentioned in comments as a fascinating but more exotic material Robert prefers to save for later

items.t: Wave Power - A New Way Forward?
topics: Evolution from the "perpetual battery" (patent #4153757 by William Clark) to a carbon-only design

Surface area as a key parameter for power output

materials: **Carbon rods** — salvaged from zinc-carbon (lantern) batteries; primary electrodes
**Salt water** — tap water with table salt, used to simulate seawater in the lab
**Wax** — present in battery-grade carbon rods as a binder; can be burned out to improve conductivity
**Clay and graphite** — combined and baked to make high-surface-area clay tablets as an alternative electrode material
**KB (Kelvin) blocks** — plastic terminal blocks used as the structural housing for the rods

**Nylon block** — used in the earlier "perpetual battery" construction
**5.1 kΩ resistor** — used as a load for current measurement

items.t: Perpetual Battery - Powered By Water
topics: Discovery of the patent via research into seawater/graphene generators and carbon conductors in flowing liquids

items.t: Refrigerators From Rubber Bands
topics: The mechanocaloric (elastocaloric) effect in rubber and elastic materials

items.t: How To Generate Electricity From A Rubber Band
