**Potassium hydroxide (KOH)** — 10 g; highly caustic alkali used to dissolve the casein; sodium hydroxide or washing soda (sodium carbonate) are noted alternatives
**Sulfur (yellow)** — 10–30 g; sourced from a DIY store; controls the degree of elasticity
**Carbon** — added in a follow-up experiment to create a conductive ink
**Acetic acid / weak hydrochloric acid / weak sulfuric acid** — for precipitating casein from raw milk (if not using powder)
**Glycerine** — mentioned in comments as an additive to improve the material

items.t: EZ Water For Solar Cells And Water Purification
topics: Live demonstration of current and voltage generation from deionized water and carbon ink
Gerald Pollack's EZ (Exclusion Zone) water theory — water as a fourth, semicrystalline state
Hydrophilic surfaces and their role in forming structured water zones
Magnetically aligned ink as a macroscale method to access EZ water effects
Light as an input to expand the EZ zone and increase charge separation
Potential dual applications: energy harvesting and water purification/desalination
materials: **Deionized / distilled water** — used as the electrolyte medium in the cell
**Carbon ink ("swag ink")** — magnetically dried onto plastic, forms the current-collecting lines
**Copper strips** — used as electrodes; two strips used to eliminate galvanic effects
**Aluminium strip** — paired with copper to introduce work-function directionality
**Methylene blue** — added as a dye to visualize water flow under the microscope; also dramatically increased current output (~2 µA to ~11–30 µA)
**PET overhead transparencies** — used as the substrate for the ink/electrode assembly

items.t: Printable Graphene Diode
topics: Graphene dispersion prepared using a blender and soap
Diode behavior in dried graphene sheets under DC bias
Effect of graphene line orientation (parallel vs. perpendicular to copper strips) on diode performance
Comparison of graphene ink vs. plain graphene solution
Potential for a printable graphene diode
materials: **Graphene** — homemade dispersion made with a blender and soap, approximately 2 months old
**Graphene ink** — used in a comparison sheet; parallel orientation tested
**Soap** — used as a dispersant in graphene preparation
**Copper strips** — deposited on plastic substrate as electrodes
**Plastic** — used as the substrate for the graphene sheet

items.t: SWAG Update - Thoughts On How The Magnet Works
topics: Why graphene and graphite are diamagnetic and how this property is exploited
How random graphene particle orientation creates high resistance (tortuous current path)
How magnetic field alignment creates direct, low-resistance paths between electrodes
How a multi-pole magnetic chuck-style device creates the non-uniform field needed
Why a solenoid (uniform field) would NOT work for this application
Why adding external magnets or complex electromagnets defeats the cost/simplicity goals of the SWAG
Design philosophy: cheap, reproducible, scalable for coastal deployment
materials: **Graphene** — diamagnetic material used as the active layer in the SWAG generator
**Graphite** — also diamagnetic; behaves similarly to graphene in a magnetic field
**Amorphous carbon** — mentioned as a contrast; NOT diamagnetic and would not respond to a magnetic field
**Salt water** — the energy input fluid flowing over the graphene layer
**Steel** — used in magnetic chuck construction as a permeable flux path
**Brass** — used as a non-permeable spacer in magnetic chuck devices
**N50 magnets** — neodymium permanent magnets mentioned as an option (but discouraged for cost reasons)

items.t: Manipulating Conductive Ink With Magnetic Fields
topics: Conductive ink aligning to magnetic field lines when wet, locking in place on drying
Use of permanent magnets (printer's block demonstrated) to shape ink patterns
Possibility of using electromagnets and solenoids to create varied shapes
Directional conductivity and its implications for heat conduction, electrical conduction, and piezoelectricity
Potential for diode-like behaviour from directionally aligned ink
Antenna applications and solar energy harvesting via wave transformation
MIM (metal-insulator-metal) diodes for rectification at high frequencies
materials: Conductive ink (magnetically responsive, used in SWAG generator context)
PET sheets (sold by NOBO as overhead transparency sheets) — used as substrate
Fumed silica — suggested as an additive to increase nanoscale surface area
Permanent magnets (printer's block used as field source)

items.t: SWAG Update - Poling In A Magnetic Field
topics: SWAG (conductive ink) generator experiments
Effect of magnetic field alignment on nanoparticle ink structure
Comparison of three electrode/field-line configurations
Measurement of current (milliamps) and voltage output
Visual demonstration of magnetic field line formation in the ink
materials: **SWAG ink** — the conductive nanoparticle-based ink under investigation
**Sodium chloride (salt)** — dissolved in water as the electrolyte test solution (600 ml water + 1 teaspoon salt)
**Aluminium chloride** — mentioned as the salt used in the original SWAG experiment at high concentration
**Copper and aluminium** — planned as current-collecting electrodes for one-way flow
**Plastic sheet** — used as a substrate on the magnet block
**NOBO laserjet transparency sheets (PET)** — mentioned in comments as the substrate material

items.t: The Effect Of Some Dopants On The New Type Of Solar Cell
topics: Comparative testing of undoped vs. copper-doped vs. manganese-doped solar cells
Effect of dopant type on photocurrent output
Performance under indoor fluorescent lighting vs. a 100W incandescent lamp
Paper as a substrate for the solar cell
materials: **Zinc sulfide nanoparticles** — the primary active material in the solar cell
**Graphene** — mixed with zinc sulfide nanoparticles as part of the cell composite
**Copper** — used as a 3% dopant in one sample
**Manganese** — used as a 3% dopant in another sample; also referenced in comments as manganese sulphate (soluble, used in fabrication) vs. manganese oxide (insoluble, found in batteries)
**Fumed silica** — mentioned in comments as a candidate additive to increase surface area
**Paper** — used as the substrate for all three samples

items.t: Effect Of Magnetic Field On Transparent Solar Cell
topics: Graphene's diamagnetic properties and their practical implications
Magnetic field alignment of graphene flakes during solution drying
Effect of graphene flake orientation on electrical conductivity in a thin-film solar cell
Comparative testing of two electrode orientations (0° and 90° relative to field lines)
materials: **Graphene** — active material in the transparent solar cell, noted as diamagnetic
**Graphene solution** — applied thinly by drop-casting onto substrates
**Copper strip** — used as one electrode
**Aluminium strip** — used as the other electrode
**Tape** — used to mask/contain the solution during deposition
**Printer's magnet** — a multi-pole alignment magnet (used in typesetting) serving as the magnetic bed

items.t: Graphene Transparent, Flexible Brand New Solar Cell
topics: Limitations of traditional solar cells (rigidity, fragility, expensive transparent conductive oxides)
Rethinking solar cell geometry: replacing flat panels with a distributed internal wire network
Using work function differences between copper and aluminium electrodes to enforce current directionality
Role of graphene as a fast charge-transport medium within the active layer
Nanofibrious cellulose as a conductive binder
Outdoor sunlight testing vs. fluorescent light performance
Speculation on 3D bulk configurations (embedding the material in a gel)
materials: **Graphene** — added at 5% (1 g/L solution, 100 ml) as the charge-transport network
**Bis thiourea zinc formate** — the photoactive material (clarified in comments); 5 ml added to the mix
**Nanofibrious cellulose** — conductive binder at 5 g/L, 100 ml
**Copper** — one electrode strip; chosen for its work function
**Aluminium** — second electrode strip; paired with copper for rectifying directionality
**Plastic sheet** — flexible substrate
**Tin oxide** — cited as the conventional transparent conductive oxide (TCO) used in commercial cells, noted as expensive and fragile
**Gum arabic** — mentioned in comments as an alternative binder valued for its "green" credentials

items.t: How To Make A Graphene Gas Sensor
topics: Construction of a graphene-based gas sensor using copper strips
Resistance-based gas detection mechanism (linear response)
Live demonstration with ammonia and exhaled breath (CO₂ + moisture)
Reversibility of the sensor signal as gases desorb
Practical considerations for building electronics around such a sensor
materials: **Graphene (rGO — reduced graphene oxide)**: the active sensing layer; confirmed in comments to be microwave-reduced
**Copper strips**: used as electrodes on either side of the graphene layer
**Ammonia**: used as a test gas; caused a ~400 MΩ resistance drop
**Carbon dioxide**: present in exhaled breath; contributes to resistance drop
**Moisture (water vapour)**: also present in breath; contributes to resistance drop

items.t: Copper Plating A Sponge
topics: Making a non-conductive sponge electrically conductive using conductive ink
Electroplating the sponge with copper using a copper sulfate/sulfuric acid bath
Removing the nylon substrate with concentrated sulfuric acid to yield a pure copper sponge
Applications of copper sponges (heat exchangers, battery plates)
Why copper must be plated first before using the Lacey safe electroplating solution
materials: **Nylon scouring sponge** — the substrate being plated
**Water-resistant conductive ink** — used to coat the sponge and make it conductive prior to plating
**Copper sulfate (100 g)** — dissolved in 500 mL water as the copper ion source in the plating bath
**Concentrated sulfuric acid, 98% (26 mL)** — added to the plating bath as electrolyte; also used neat to dissolve the nylon after plating
**Battery acid** — mentioned as a lower-concentration alternative (use 3× the volume)
**Lacey solution** — a safe electroplating solution for copper and other metals; noted that it will not plate directly onto carbon, only onto copper

items.t: The Birth Of A New Type Of Solar Cell ?
topics: Serendipitous discovery: how a lab mistake led to a new solar cell concept
Synthesis of bis-thiourea zinc formate and its thermal decomposition to zinc sulfide
Zinc sulfide nanoparticles and their photoactive/reversible reduction behaviour
MIM (Metal-Insulator-Metal) diode theory and band diagrams
Wave-based vs. particle-based models of solar energy collection
Work functions of metals and their role in MIM diode design

materials: **Formic acid (85%)** — starting material for zinc formate synthesis
**Zinc oxide** — reacted with formic acid to produce zinc formate
**Zinc formate** — intermediate compound, purified by recrystallisation
**Thiourea** — combined with zinc formate in a 2:1 molar ratio; used in photography
**Bis-thiourea zinc formate** — the key precursor; not commercially available, must be synthesised; decomposes to zinc sulfide at ~400°C
**Zinc sulfide (ZnS) nanoparticles** — the photoactive insulating layer; glow-in-the-dark material; insoluble, formed by accident when lacy plating solution was added
**Lacy plating solution** (oxalic acid, ammonium sulfate, tri-sodium phosphate) — accidentally introduced, triggering ZnS nanoparticle formation
**Copper** — electrode (higher work function metal)
**Aluminium** — electrode (lower work function metal)
**Titanium dioxide (TiO2)** — added experimentally; boosted output to ~25–26 µA
**Deionised water** — solvent and electrolyte
**Graphite (mechanical graphing sheet)** — used as counter electrode in one test cell

items.t: Some Furthur Thoughts On Constructing A Giant Radiator
topics: Managing expectations for first-time builders of the conductive ink radiator
The importance of practice before attempting the large-scale version
How environmental conditions (humidity, temperature) affect ink absorbency and usage

Ink quantity considerations (15 mL vs 25 mL quantities)
materials: **Conductive ink** — applied by roller to a board to create a resistive heating element; available in 15 mL and 25 mL quantities

items.t: Further Thoughts On Selling Giant Wall Heaters
topics: Recap of the giant wall radiator / conductive heating ink concept
UK and European electrical regulations (IET Wiring Regulations 17th Edition)
"Competent person" requirements for mains-connected installations
Class 2 (double insulation) safety classification for electrical appliances
Product safety certification and the external lab testing process
Materials Safety Data Sheets (MSDS) — cost and process
Standalone appliance route vs. direct mains installation
Carbon thin film heater technology as the mature predecessor to this work
Encouragement for entrepreneurial use of the technology despite regulatory hurdles
materials: **Conductive/heat-resistive ink** — the core material painted onto walls or substrates to create radiant heaters; Robert notes it took ~1.5 years to develop and ~1 year to certify
**Carbon thin film** — existing mature technology (carbon pin film heaters) used for underfloor heating, identified as the basis for the wall heater concept

items.t: Painting A Giant Radiator Straight On Your Wall
topics: Concept of painting a radiant heater directly onto a wall or floor using conductive ink
Surface preparation (plasterboard/drywall substrate)
Copper tape electrode layout and electrical connections
Thermocouple (temperature sensor) installation and embedding in wall filler
Resistance measurement to determine power output (Ohm's law applied)

Wiring to mains supply (UK colour codes: brown = live, blue = neutral, bare = earth)
Electrical safety, UK wiring regulations, and Class II device classification
Covering the finished heater (vinyl, tiles, carpet, skirting board)

materials: **Heat-resistive / carbon-based conductive ink** — the core heating element, painted onto the substrate; Robert notes he made his own and did a separate video on how to make it
**Copper tape** — used as electrode bus bars along the edges; approximately 5 metres long, 1 cm wide per run; must lie flat and be painted over to ensure good carbon-to-copper junction
**Plasterboard (drywall)** — substrate for the heater panel (½ metre × 2.4 metre sheet)
**Rockwool / insulation board** — mentioned as optional backing/insulation material
**Wall filler (spackle/compound)** — used to embed the thermocouple sensor just below the surface
**Masking tape** — used to define painting zones and panel geometry
**Plastic sheeting / vinyl** — protective cover over the live heating surface
**Earth sleeving (green/yellow)** — used to safely terminate the earth wire where no earth connection is required (Class II device)
**Sodium silicate** — mentioned in comments as a suitable fireproof binder for making resistive ink at higher temperatures
**Kapton film** — mentioned in comments as a high-temperature (up to 400°C) alternative covering material

items.t: Terry Hope - Casting Thin, Tough Bioplastics Casein

materials: **Casein** — protein extracted from milk or purchased as sodium caseinate; the primary casting material

**Chalk** — additive for increased rigidity
**Clay** — additive for increased rigidity
**Kevlar fibers** — additive for toughness
**Conductive ink** — blended into casein to produce thin conductive sheets
**Ammonium ferric citrate** — mentioned in comments as a material Robert was beginning to experiment with alongside gelatin

items.t: Do You Have A Question? - Part of The League Of Independant Scientists

items.t: Making Fake Ivory And Casein Milk Plastics, Glues and Paints

**Citric acid** — used as the precipitating acid (1 tsp in 100mL water); vinegar and lemon juice cited as alternatives

**Formalin (40% formaldehyde solution)** — used to cross-link and harden the final casein plastic; discussed with safety caveats
**Glycerol** — mentioned in comments as a humectant additive to control plasticity
**Honey** — mentioned in comments as an alternative humectant
**Graphite** — used to stain the imitation ivory piece in the demonstration

items.t: How To Improve Your YouTube Ranking

items.t: How To Make A Gelatin Foam

items.t: Graphene From Green Tea

items.t: On The League Of Extraordinarily Mad Scientists

items.t: Copper Nanoparticles In Gelatin

items.t: Graphene From Tea

items.t: Easy Way To Cut Complex Shapes On Sheet Glass - Glass Cutting Ink

items.t: Conductive Ink Pen For Paper Circuits

items.t: What Is Chemistry - Atoms, Bonds And Molecules

items.t: Copper Plating Without A Battery

items.t: How To Reduce Graphene Oxide To Graphene With Photonic Curing

items.t: How To Make Copper Nanoparticles Suitable For Ink Jet Printing and Photonic Sintering

items.t: Essential Guidance Before Posting A Comment On My Channel

materials: **Graphene / few-layer graphene oxide** — used as an example of a problem-specific material; made as large-area sheets for supercapacitors and water/ethanol purification membranes, not as a theoretically "pure" single-atom graphene layer
**Aluminium** — cited as an example of a theoretically ideal but practically impossible substrate (infinitely long, perfectly smooth)

**Hand wash / soap ingredients** — broken down by function: ~90% water, plus concentrated liquid actives; Robert sought green, non-caustic, low-water alternatives for the Echoo

items.t: Graphene - An Introductory Course
topics: What graphene is: a single-layer 2D sheet of hexagonally arranged carbon atoms
Carbon allotropes and the dimensional schema: graphite (3D), carbon nanotubes (1D), fullerenes (0D), graphene (2D)
The original Scotch-tape discovery method (Andre Geim's work)
Top-down vs. bottom-up production approaches
Graphite oxide / graphene oxide route as the most accessible top-down method
Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) on copper or nickel foil
Bottom-up synthesis from polyaromatic hydrocarbon precursors (sugar, benzene, pitch, tar)
How graphite powder particle size affects end-product properties (small powder → solution casting; large powder → gel/fiber spinning)
Bridging grain boundaries with carbon nanotubes or metal nanowires
The LightScribe laser method for making supercapacitors
Applications: supercapacitors, batteries, transistors, solar cells, fuel cells, water filtration, transparent conductive screens, strong fibers
Graphene modification: N-type doping (phosphorus) and P-type doping to create photovoltaic junctions
3D graphene foam/sponge structures and their surface-area advantages
The principle of "know your materials" as a guiding philosophy throughout the course
materials: **Graphite** — bulk 3D precursor material; found in pencils
**Graphene** — single-layer hexagonal carbon sheet; the target product
**Graphite oxide / graphene oxide** — oxidised intermediate used in the top-down route; water-dispersible
**Cellulose tape (Scotch/Sellotape)** — used in the original mechanical exfoliation discovery method
**Copper foil** — catalyst substrate for CVD; absorbs carbon when hot, releases it on cooling
**Nickel foil** — alternative catalyst substrate for CVD
**Phosphorus** — used for N-type doping of graphene
**Sugar (table sugar)** — carbon precursor for bottom-up synthesis
**Benzene** — aromatic hydrocarbon precursor for bottom-up synthesis
**Toluene** — mentioned as a hydrocarbon that can drive polyaromatic condensation
**Pitch and tar** — carbon-rich precursors for bottom-up graphene synthesis
**Sodium hydroxide** — used as a coagulation bath for wet-spinning graphene oxide gel into fibers
**Carbon nanotubes** — used as conductive bridges across graphene grain boundaries
**Copper metal nanowires** — used alongside graphene to bridge weak boundary points in transparent conductive films
**Helium** — cited in the context of graphene's gas impermeability (graphene-coated balloons that don't deflate)
**Water** — noted as the one substance graphene allows through, relevant to desalination

items.t: Electrochemical Cells, Entropy and Gibbs Free Energy
topics: Carnot efficiency and the thermodynamic limits of heat engines

Gibbs free energy (ΔG = ΔH − TΔS) and its three states (positive, zero, negative)
Enthalpy and how it is measured (bomb calorimeter)
Entropy: critique of the "disorder" interpretation; Robert's preferred framing as energy required to maintain structural arrangement
The second law of thermodynamics — its scope, limitations, and dependence on entropy
The distinction between laws as *descriptions* of systems versus laws as *constraints* on systems
The applicability of thermodynamic laws only to closed systems, and uncertainty about whether the universe is closed
materials: Steam (transfer fluid of the industrial revolution)
Petrol/gasoline (transfer fluid of internal combustion engines)

items.t: Is Free Energy Possible

Crystal radio sets as an example of a narrowly-viewed free energy device
Solar panels, wind turbines, and sailboats as mainstream free energy systems
Coefficient of Performance (COP) as the proper metric instead of simple efficiency
Dark energy, dark matter, and quantum foam as a potential energy reservoir
The universe as a "sea of potential energy" that structures can be built to tap
materials: None mentioned.

items.t: The Problem With Science
topics: Science as an unacknowledged belief system, comparable in structure to religion
Berkeley's argument that human senses are inherently unreliable
The "lens theory": all perception — including via instruments — is filtered through fallible human senses
Bertrand Russell's challenge to the existence of matter: we only measure fields, never matter itself
The Cartesian mind-body problem: why do mental ideas (e.g. cannonball trajectories) map onto physical reality?
Economics 101 as an analogy for sciences built on false foundational precepts (e.g. infinite consumption)
Falsificationism in theory vs. the entrenchment of academic consensus in practice
Paradigm shifts: rare, costly to individuals who trigger them, and resisted by institutional self-interest
Science's tendency toward mystification through jargon as a gatekeeping mechanism
The suppression of questioning once laws are established
Human nature (noble vs. ignoble) and how it shapes scientific institutions
Redefining progress as understanding and exploration rather than material accumulation
The "investigator" as a healthier model than the credentialled "scientist"
materials: Water (hot, cold, and medium temperature) — used in Berkeley's three-tub sensory illusion demonstration

items.t: What To Do About The Patent System
topics: The corruption and dysfunction of the modern patent system
Andre Geim's (graphene Nobel laureate) decision not to patent, and why
Software and biotech patents becoming increasingly untenable
The psychology of wanting wealth vs. actually needing "a comfortable wedge"
Robert's personal philosophy of openness: publishing ideas openly to attract collaboration and work
The concept of "leap of faith" — trusting that people are fundamentally decent
Shared stakeholder model: everyone involved in developing an idea deserves a share of the reward
Pharmaceutical companies and exploitative drug pricing as a symptom of patent abuse
Recognition and respect from peers as more valuable than money
materials: **Graphene** — mentioned as the landmark discovery by Andre Geim that sparked the discussion about whether to patent a breakthrough material
**Pharmaceutical drugs** — referenced in the context of companies charging £300/$300 per pill as an example of unfair monopoly pricing under patent protection

items.t: The Health And Safety Police   or Health and Safety Gone Mad - A Rant

items.t: All Against Patents   Why Patents Are A Bad Idea
topics: Why Robert personally does not patent his ideas
The **practical objection**: high cost of filing (UK registration ~£120, worldwide protection costs tens of thousands), patents as a "red flag" inviting theft, and the prohibitive cost of litigation

The **moral objection**: patent trolling (companies patenting unimplemented ideas to sue later), an overwhelmed patent system unable to properly examine applications, and duplicate patents being granted (e.g., the Hummer method)
The **humanitarian objection**: patents actively slow down scientific and technological progress
Historical example: James Watt's steam engine patents delayed development by ~20 years
Contemporary example: graphene research being impeded by patent confusion and trolling
Robert's self-described role as a supplier of "nuts and bolts" — basic building blocks others use to achieve great things
Channel moderation policy
materials: **Graphene** — used as a primary example of a field plagued by patent trolling and overcrowding (1,000–2,000 patents/month, 3,000–4,000 articles/month); also cited for the re-granting of the expired Hummer method patent
**Carbon foam** — mentioned as one of Robert's own inventions, cited as an example of a building-block material that enabled another person's idea for an inertial electrostatic confinement device

**Potassium** — mentioned as an alternative intercalant for making N-type GIC

materials: **Sand** (builder's sand, ~3500 grain size) — the base filtration substrate
**Graphene oxide solution** (stock at 10 mg/mL, diluted to ~0.4% by weight / ~4 mg/mL) — the active coating
**Deionized water** — used to dilute the graphene oxide to working concentration

**Sodium borohydride (NaBH₄)** — alternative reducing agent for graphene oxide; also used to reduce silver nitrate
**Silver nitrate (AgNO₃)** — used to grow metallic silver nanoparticles on graphene oxide for antibacterial effect

items.t: Paper Electronics 2 - A Basic Stylophone

**Paper / card** — substrate for the circuit

items.t: Paper Electronics 1 - Simple Light Circuit

items.t: Introduction to Making Electronic Components from Conductive Ink and the 555

materials: **Conductive ink** (homemade) — the primary medium for drawing components; ~40 ohms/cm resistivity
**Paper** — substrate for painting circuits; dielectric constant ~3.85

**Card stock / birthday card** — used as capacitor dielectric; thickness ~0.254 mm

items.t: Conductive Ink For Electroplating Leaves

items.t: How To Make a Ball Mill 2 - A High Energy Ball Mill

materials: **4-inch stainless steel pipe** — used as the body/drum of the ball mill

**Builder's board (PVC with polyurethane foam core)** — material for conical end plugs

items.t: How To Make A Variable Speed Ball Mill From A Drill

**Zirconium balls** — grinding medium option
**Stainless steel ball bearings** (various sizes) — grinding medium and structural components
**Flint pebbles** (1 cm down to ~2 mm diameter) — low-cost grinding medium alternative
**Inline skate bearings** (8 mm ID, 22 mm OD) — roller end bearings
**Rubber/inner tube material** — wrapped around drum to improve grip on rollers
**Electrical insulation tape** — used to correct drum precession
**Graphite + dry ice** — mentioned in comments as inputs for making graphene oxide
**Aluminium/graphite composites** — example application (piezoelectric composites)
**Nickel hydroxide-doped graphene** — stated as Robert's personal intended use for the mill

items.t: Ultrasand - water desalination and purification using graphene coated sand

items.t: Copper nanoparticles for conductive inks by water and polyol synthesis
topics: Why the earlier copper nanoparticle method was insufficient (oxidation, settling, no capping agent)
Role of PVP (polyvinylpyrrolidone) as a capping and stabilising agent

materials: **Copper acetate** (copper(II) acetate) — primary copper salt used; copper sulfate or copper nitrate noted as alternatives
**Ascorbic acid (vitamin C)** — reducing agent; purchased food-grade in kilogram sacks
**PVP (polyvinylpyrrolidone, MW ~100,000 g/mol)** — capping/stabilising agent; MW 111 g/mol used for molar calculations based on monomer unit

**Cu₂O (copper(I) oxide)** — intermediate species observed as bright orange-yellow during reaction

items.t: Green Reduction of Graphene Oxide to Graphene

items.t: Improved Faster Method for Making Graphene Oxide
topics: Motivation for the update: improved method and better video/audio quality
Overview of the three-stage synthesis: cold stage, medium temperature stage, high temperature stage
Safety hazards, particularly the formation of manganese heptoxide (explosive) if temperature control is lost
Practical tips for sourcing reagents (garden stores, online, hardware shops)
Filtration and washing procedure to purify the final product
Bath sonication to disperse the graphene oxide cake
Discussion of graphite particle size and its effect on paste consistency and yield

materials: **Graphite powder (5 g)** — starting material; available from garden stores as fertiliser

**Sodium nitrate / sodium nitrite (2.5 g)** — also called "nitrate of soda" or Chile saltpetre; available as fertiliser or food preservative; Robert notes the Tour group believes it contributes little, but he thinks it helps pre-oxidise the graphite
**Sulfuric acid, 98% concentrated (115 mL)** — available as drain cleaner ("One Shot instant drain cleaner"); minimum usable concentration ~94%
**Potassium permanganate (15 g)** — added slowly in powdered form during cold stage; forms explosive manganese heptoxide in solution if temperature exceeds ~55 °C
**Water (230 mL + 400 mL, totalling ~630 mL)** — added in two separate stages
**Hydrogen peroxide, 30–35% (50 mL)** — terminates the reaction; food-grade available online
**Hydrochloric acid, 1 molar (~100 mL)** — used to wash out residual metal salts (potassium, sodium) from the filter cake; available as "spirit of salts" from builders' merchants
**Deionised water (~800 mL)** — final wash to bring filtrate pH to ~6–7

items.t: Dissolving Graphite to get Graphene - Liquid Phase Exfoliation

Why non-polar organic solvents can exfoliate graphene without destroying it

materials: **Graphite** — starting material; raw graphite and expanded/intercalated graphite both discussed
**Expanded (exfoliated/intercalated) graphite** — preferred starting material (~200x expanded, sheets much further apart)
**NMP (N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone)** — first solvent historically used for liquid phase exfoliation

items.t: Microwave Exfoliation of Intercalated Graphite

items.t: High diamagnetic graphite solar engine - part 1

items.t: Making Zinc Acetate

items.t: Hydrogen Fuel Cell

items.t: Air Stable Iron nanoparticles from green tea and HTC

items.t: Graphene from table sugar - the Tang Lau Method

items.t: Green Graphene

items.t: Simple Automata - Rocking ship sculpture from driftwood - part 1
topics: Concept and design of a driftwood automata depicting a rocking ship
Selecting and preparing driftwood pieces for the sculpture
Cutting driftwood into three functional components: cliff face, wave panel, and boat
Shaping the wave panel using drilled holes and a hacksaw
Colouring wood with oil paint
Assembling the cliff/backing section using dowels
Shaping the small boat piece with a chimney and crank hole
materials: Driftwood (likely from a groyne/coastal structure)
French ultramarine artists' oil paint (used as a wood stain/colourant)
6mm wooden dowels (structural joinery and chimney detail)
4mm drill bit / hole (for crank shaft fitment)
10mm (1cm) drill bit (for wave cutouts)

items.t: CartaPesta or papier mache sculpture for the classroom - part 3
topics: Applying wet newspaper strips to a foam sculpted armature
Allowing paper to fall and fold naturally to preserve vitality in the finished piece
Filling gaps between paper and foam with polyfiller to achieve a carved-stone appearance
Finishing with black paint (black ground/primer) followed by metallic wax
Using a paper-and-PVA pulp mixture for sculptural detail work (sanding and carving)
Suitability of the technique for classroom use with teenagers
materials: **Newspaper** — torn/cut strips soaked in PVA mix for applying to the armature
**PVA glue** (50/50 mix with water) — adhesive binder for the paper layers
**Wallpaper paste** — mentioned as a possible alternative to PVA (untested by Robert)
**Flexible gap polyfiller** — used to fill recessed gaps between paper and foam so the surface reads as carved stone
**Black paint / black ground** — base coat applied after filling
**Treasure Silver** — a coloured metallic wax applied over highlights; available in arts and crafts shops
**Treasure Gold** — a gold variant of the same metallic wax, used on a more elaborate example piece
**Foam** — the underlying armature material

items.t: CartaPesta or Papier Mache Sculpture for the classroom - Part 2
topics: Refining the mould/cast head with sandpaper before use
Proportioning the figure (8:1 body-to-head ratio for elegance)
Constructing the body armature from rolled newspaper cones
Shaping the dress, torso, and chest with newspaper and tissue
Forming arms from folded and twisted paper
Using masking tape to hold the armature together
Classroom context: scaling the technique for groups of 30 students
materials: Newspaper (for cones, body bulk, and arms)
Masking tape (primary binding material; noted as compatible with later glue)
Tissue paper (for subtle chest shaping)
Plasticine (mentioned in context of the Part 1 mould)
Fibreglass rod / hanger wire / stick (central armature rod)
Doll's head (used as the face cast from Part 1)

items.t: CartaPesta or Papier Mache sculpture for the classroom - Part 1
topics: History and origin of Carta Pesta from Lecce, southern Italy
Using a doll's head as a reference for face casting
Making a plasticine impression mold of a face
Casting a face using resin mixed with flour as a filler
Cleaning, carving, and sanding the cast to refine features
Filling air bubble holes with household/wood filler
materials: **Plasticine** — used to make the impression mold of the doll's face
**Alfred's Fast Glass resin** — casting resin used with a hardener to form the face
**Resin hardener** (red-colored) — mixed with the resin; ratio affects set speed and brittleness
**Plain flour** — added to the resin as a filler to make it carvable rather than glass-brittle
**Wood filler / household filler** — used to fill air bubble holes in the cast surface
**Sandpaper** — for smoothing lumps on the cast face

items.t: How to Build a Van Der Graaf Generator - Part 5b - Rollers and motor
topics: Disassembly of a belt sander to harvest parts
Identification and extraction of usable rollers (top and bottom)
Extraction of motor components: rotor, stator, brushes/bushes, commutator
Repurposing the sander's motor housing for the Van de Graaff build
Preparation of the bottom roller assembly (removing rubber coating, retaining drive gear and pulley)
materials: Aluminium (bottom roller material beneath rubber coating)
Rubber (glued coating on the aluminium roller, removed by chisel while chucked in a drill)
Teflon (mentioned as a possible future coating for a roller)
Brass (bush/brush mountings)
Plastic (roller housing, shoe plate)
Steel (shoe plate)

items.t: How to build a Van Der Graaf Generator - Part 5a - Rollers and motor
topics: Disassembling a broken belt sander to harvest its motor and rollers
Identifying and extracting reusable components (rollers, pulley, roller bearings, fan, motor)
Plans to coat the rollers with different materials to position them correctly on the triboelectric series
Cost-effectiveness of using a cheap belt sander as a Van De Graaff drive mechanism
materials: Plastic rollers (from the belt sander, to be coated with triboelectrically appropriate material)
Melted rubber fan belt (identified as the cause of the sander's failure)
Roller bearing (salvaged from the sander internals)

items.t: How to Build a Van Der Graaf Generator - Part 4 - Making the toroid
topics: Sourcing and sizing vacuum cleaner hose for the toroid form
Bending and gluing the hose into a toroid shape around the support pipe
Filling surface ridges with polyester builder's resin (Seifix) for a smooth finish
Applying aluminium ducting tape in two layers (inner and outer strips) to metallize the surface
Burnishing and polishing the tape for a clean finish
Brief digression on adapting the technique for a Tesla coil toroid
Purpose of the toroid: preventing high-voltage creepage down the support column
materials: **Vacuum cleaner hose (~45 mm diameter)** — sourced from an old Dyson, used as the toroid body
**Seifix polyester resin** — a builder's bolt-fixing compound used to fill the ridges of the corrugated hose; described as containing sand, being sticky and flexible
**Super glue** — used to join the ends of the hose
**Aluminium ducting tape** — used in 10 cm rolls (also available in 6 cm); applied in inner and outer strips to metallize the surface
**Car body filler** — mentioned as an alternative to Seifix, with the caveat that it must be sealed before taping

items.t: How to Build a Van Der Graaf Generator - Part 3 - the base
topics: Overview of the completed basic structure including the steel gazing ball toroid top
Relationship between the toroid radius and maximum voltage output
Sources of charge leakage and how to minimise them (especially around the bottom hole of the sphere)
Construction of the base from builder's board
M12 threaded rod leg system with square plate washers and dome nuts
Tilt/alignment correction mechanism for belt tracking
materials: Steel gazing ball (used as the toroid top terminal)
Builder's board (MDF/chipboard, ~175 mm wide)
M12 threaded rod (45 cm lengths)
Square plate washers
Dome nuts
Sandpaper / emery paper (for deburring)

items.t: How to build a Van der Graaf Generator - Part 2 - Making the tower
topics: Cutting the main tower pipe (drainpipe/conduit) to length
Design and spacing of stabilising fins along the tower
Why timber is unsuitable for high-voltage builds (it conducts at high voltage)
Using uPVC foam board ("builders board") as the primary structural material
Cutting circular holes in the fins using a variable hole cutter or DIY scoring tool
Gluing fins to the pipe with cyanoacrylate (superglue)
Base plate construction and lateral stability
materials: **PVC drainpipe/conduit** — 110 mm diameter, 60 cm length; forms the main tower column
**Builders board (uPVC coated foam board)** — used for fins and base plate; preferred over timber for its insulating properties at high voltage
**Cyanoacrylate (superglue)** — used to bond fins and structural components; creates an extremely strong permanent joint

items.t: How to build aVan Der Graaf Generator
topics: Comparison between a small existing Van de Graaff generator (~10,000V) and the planned large build (~300,000–500,000V)
How dome size and roundness determine maximum voltage output
Core components and operating principles of a Van de Graaff generator
Triboelectric series and its role in charge separation between rollers
Faraday's law applied to charge accumulation on the dome
Parts sourcing plan for the larger build (belt sander motor, drain pipe, exercise band belt)
materials: Stainless steel garden ornament ball (large dome)
PTFE (Teflon) plumber's tape — used to wrap the top roller of the small generator
Aluminium — used for the bottom roller
Rubber exercise band (~1.2 m) — used as the belt; also planned for the large build
100 mm (4-inch) drain/sewer pipe — planned column for the large build
1.5V–9V DC motor with chain drive (from Maplin kit) — powers the small generator
Old belt sander — planned donor for motor and rollers on the large build

items.t: homemade ukulele - putting it together
topics: Correct fret spacing measurement technique
Exact fret position measurements for a 33 cm scale ukulele
Securing components with superglue and small bolts
A short demonstration of the finished instrument being played
materials: Superglue — used to hold components in place before mechanical fastening

items.t: homemade Ukulele - the neck - part 2
topics: Shaping the ukulele neck from oak floorboard
Installing frets using copper electrical wire instead of commercial fret wire
Making a nut from fiberglass offcut
String retainer/tuning head recap from earlier video
Using a 6-inch tin can as the banjo-ukulele body
Preview of the final assembly to come
materials: **Oak floorboard** — used for the neck
**Copper wire (2.5 mm cross-sectional area)** — used as improvised fret wire; the type used for socket ring mains, available from B&Q, Home Depot, or electrical stores
**4 mm fiberglass sheet** — used to make the nut
**Super glue** — used to seat frets and nut
**Teak oil** — applied as a finish to make the neck look shiny
**6-inch tin can (from a pound store)** — to be used as the banjo body

items.t: Homemade ukulele - The neck - part 1
topics: Sourcing affordable hardwood for instrument building (DIY store oak flooring)
Soprano ukulele dimensions (scale length, neck width, fret count)
Laminating thin wood strips to achieve the required neck thickness
Using machine-finished surfaces to minimise warping
Overview of the headless tuner design and string path
materials: Oak flooring strips (£5 for five strips from a local DIY store) — used as the neck material
6mm wooden dowels — used to reinforce the laminated neck layers

items.t: Gap Motor Replication
topics: Introduction to Art Porter's gapless/gap motor design (via Patrick Kelly's free energy compendium)
Scaled-down replication using small, affordable neodymium magnets
Construction details: brass bar slider, plastic tube housing, soft iron core coil
Replacing mechanical timing with a 555 astable timer and relay
Mark-space ratio adjustment for timing optimisation
Converting linear motion to rotary via a cam (Art Porter's approach vs. alternatives)
Linear alternator concept as a potential direct output method
Back-spike energy capture as a future improvement
materials: **Neodymium (neo) bar magnet** — 5 cm × 1 cm, used as the moving head magnet seated in the plastic tube
**Neodymium (neo) disc magnets** — 1 cm × 2 mm discs, stack of 15 used to replicate Art Porter's base magnet section
**4 mm brass bar** — forms the slider shaft
**6 mm plastic tube** — houses the bar magnet and fits over the brass bar
**Soft iron core (8 mm)** — fabricated from a nail, filed flat, used as the coil core
**26 gauge enamel wire** — wound thickly to form the coil
**White electrician's tape** — used to wrap and secure the finished coil
**100 µF electrolytic capacitor** — part of the 555 timer timing circuit (noted as too large; replacement with a lower-value cap planned)
**12 V battery** — power supply for the demonstration

items.t: Homemade Ukulele or headless guitar tuning head
topics: Design concept for a headless-style ukulele string anchor
Modifying electrical terminal blocks for use as string holders
Soldering brass components together with proper technique
Spacing calculations for ukulele string layout
Integration of the anchor with the ukulele neck/nut
materials: **Electrical terminal block** (5-amp and 3-amp) — used as the base for string-clamping inserts
**Brass bar** — 1 cm wide, ~2.3 mm thick, cut to 3.5 cm for the ukulele-sized anchor
**28-gauge magnet wire** — used to hold brass inserts in place during soldering
**Flux** — applied to brass bar before soldering
**Solder** — used to permanently fix the terminal block inserts to the brass bar

items.t: Home made Ukulele or headless guitar tuning head
topics: The problem with common DIY tuning peg solutions (wooden pegs, bolt/nut pegs) not holding tune
Inspiration from headless guitar tuning head designs
Step-by-step fabrication of a sliding wing-nut tuning mechanism
Sizing and fitting four tuners into a single aluminium channel assembly
Cost breakdown and sourcing of parts
materials: **4mm wing nuts** — modified to act as the string-catching tuning element
**M4 × 70mm pan head bolts** — the threaded rod that drives the wing nut along the channel
**Aluminium heat sink / cooling fin** (salvaged from an old television) — cut into guide channels and a back plate
**Super glue** — used to fix the back plate to the channel assembly
